EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving upper level disturbance passing southward along the Rocky Mountains brought a potent cold front that surged south through eastern NM. High winds and light to moderate snow accompanied this storm system. High wind gusts greater than 60 mph were observed at Raton and through portions of Union County. The combination of strong winds and blowing snow also caused several road closures and crashes along U.S. Highway 64 from Raton to Clayton, and along I-40 in the Central Highlands. An isolated snowfall accumulation of 5 inches in DeBaca County were reported as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station southwest of Clayton measured a peak wind gust of 65 mph, with a few other gusts topping 60 mph during this timeframe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two sequential winter storm systems rolled through northern and central New Mexico on February 13, 2023 and February 14, 2023. Heavy snow was reported across much of the northern mountains of New Mexico, as well as a few isolated spots in and around the Santa Fe and Albuquerque metro areas by the morning of February 14, 2023. Snow accumulations reaching 10 to 11 inches were reported atop the Sangre de Cristo Mountains, while severe winter driving conditions were reported on U.S. Highway 64 in the Tusas Mountains. Strong westerly winds filled in quickly as the main upper level low exited the area during the morning of February 14, 2023. High winds with peak gusts of 60 to 70 mph were reported through the southwest mountains, Sandia and Manzano Mountains, and over a large area of the eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public weather station measured a 62 mph wind gust at the top of South Baldy in the Magdalena Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively-tilted, shortwave trough across the Four Corners brought lee troughing across the Southern Plains. A dryline was positioned across portions of west Texas and southeast New Mexico. Many splitting supercells developed along the dryline, producing large hail and damaging winds.
Continued large-scale troughing and a weak disturbance resulted in strong to severe thunderstorms along the higher terrain of southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured severe winds with a vehicle-mounted anemometer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued lee troughing resulting from a large-scale upper-low across the western CONUS brought additional thunderstorms to the area. Afternoon and evening thunderstorms formed along the higher elevations and tracked eastward producing reports of large hail and damaging winds.

Continued lee troughing resulting from a large-scale upper-low across the western CONUS brought additional thunderstorms to the area. Afternoon and evening thunderstorms formed along the higher elevations and tracked eastward producing reports of large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported sustained winds of 53 MPH with gusts to 66 MPH from vehicle anemometer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A digging shortwave trough over the Rocky Mountains caused widespread thunderstorms across the region. Most thunderstorms were sub-severe but a lone supercell produced two brief tornadoes over northern Lea County with an additional severe gust to the south in Big Bend National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter video showed a tornado briefly touched down in an open field north of Tatum, New Mexico. No damage was reported. Path details are estimations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A digging shortwave trough over the Rocky Mountains caused widespread thunderstorms across the region. Most thunderstorms were sub-severe but a lone supercell produced two brief tornadoes over northern Lea County with an additional severe gust to the south in Big Bend National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser shared a photo of a cone tornado buried in the rain north of US Highway 380 and just west of State Road 769. No damage was reported and the duration was unknown. Path details are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large western US trough brought continued lee troughing to the Great Plains. This active pattern provided ample wind shear and instability for severe weather. Numerous long-track supercells developed during the afternoon and evening across a large portion of west Texas and southeast New Mexico. These thunderstorms produced numerous large hail, some very large, and severe wind reports. Additionally, flash flooding occurred due to slow-moving high-precipitation supercells.
A weak surface trough extended up along the Rio Grande Valley with very dry air in place at the low levels. Virga showers were able to develop and produced a dry microburst north of Radium Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Mesonet location at the CDRRC Camp located 6 miles north-northeast of Radium Springs recorded a peak gust of 61 mph in a dry microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In an unusual mid-May pattern, an upper high over the southeast United States brought a weak, but deep southeast flow to southern New Mexico.  Moisture was deep enough to produce some locally heavy rain and even some near severe thunderstorms over the Gila region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel sized hail one inch deep fell around the Gila Hot Springs area.  In addition to the hail, 1.30 inches of rain fell in an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low off the coast of California helped funnel in subtropical moisture into the state resulting in the development of widespread showers and storms across central and eastern New Mexico June 5th and 6th. Reports of severe weather and flash flooding stretched from the Rio Grande Valley across portions of eastern NM on June 5th. These reports ranged from a landspout tornado near the Petroglyph National Monument and Nara Visa, NM to flash flooding. Another round of severe weather began in the afternoon of June 6th producing more widespread reports over the same area of the state. Reports ranged from burn scar flash flooding within the Hermits Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar and Santa Rosa, to large hail over parts of San Miguel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public service in Chaves County reported penny size hail on roadway. Roadway was closed for reasons outside of hail accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe storms developed over Union County during the late afternoon to early evening hours coinciding with peak heating. The storms moved southeast into the Texas and Oklahoma panhandles following the northwest upper-level flow. There were multiple hail reports ranging in size from pennies to half-dollars. Hail accumulation and heavy rain in Clayton caused some minor flooding impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chaser relayed report of nickel to quarter size hail covering the ground from the first supercell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several consecutive days of significant severe weather impacted eastern New Mexico from May 24-26, 2023. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the dryline and east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on May 23rd. Storms tracked east and southeast across the eastern plains of New Mexico. Extensive severe weather was reported when these storms encountered greater moisture and lift closer to the Texas border. Hail up to 3.5 inches in diameter, thunderstorm wind gusts of 80 to 90 mph, and three tornados were reported in an area stretching from Union County to Roosevelt County. The largest hail stones and strongest wind gusts focused throughout Curry County during the evening hours. Hail and wind damage was reported in Tucumcari, Grady, and Clovis. Three separate tornadoes were reported in northern Curry County. Thunderstorms also dropped very heavy rainfall of 2 to 4 inches of rain across portions of Quay, De Baca, Curry, and Roosevelt Counties. Urban flooding occurred in Clovis, NM, causing some roads to be closed, and some cars to be stranded. Severe weather continued through the night and into May 25th with numerous reports of large hail, strong winds, and flash flooding. Quay County was particularly hard hit with baseball size hail and torrential rainfall amounts in excess of 5 inches. Significant hail damage was reported across Tucumcari with widespread flooding across the county. Severe wind gusts of 70 to 80 mph were common with these storms with damage reported. Strong to severe storms continued well into the night once again on the 25th with more heavy rainfall around the Caprock. The focus for severe weather shifted farther west on May 26th with large hail and heavy rainfall reported around Torrance County. A tornado and baseball size hail was also reported with a supercell near Encino. This activity spread eastward through the day with large hail, damaging wind gusts, and more torrential rainfall around the Caprock well into the night of May 26th. Three-day rainfall amounts exceeded 6 inches in many parts of Curry and Quay counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail was reported just west of San Jon, NM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor front brought moist easterly upslope flow into northeastern NM on June 11th and June 12th. Discrete supercells fired up along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains during the early afternoon hours of June 11th dropping large to destructive hail as they translated east towards the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles. More storms developed off the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains around sunset dropping more large to destructive hail across the region during the overnight hours into Monday morning. Storms eventually trained over the same areas resulting in major flash flooding over Colfax, Union, Mora and Harding Counties. More discrete supercells fired up midday June 12th tracking east before exiting into the Texas Panhandle around sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball sized hail fell with a supercell as it moved southeast through southern Union County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor front entered northeastern NM during the early morning hours increasing surface moisture across the region. This added moisture along with daytime heating allowed isolated supercells to develop across southeastern Colfax and eastern Mora counties, moving southeast towards the Texas state line. Storms produced up to golf ball sized hail in Springer and near Roy, and wind gusts of up to 74 mph at Tucumcari.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher low-level moisture on July 13th and a backdoor cold front replenishing this moisture for July 14th and 15th combined with robust upper level wind shear produced three days of severe weather across eastern NM. Severe weather impacts were limited to northeastern NM on the 13th and 14th, becoming more widespread across northeastern and east-central NM on the 15th. There were a few thunderstorm wind gusts measured in the vicinity of the White Sands Missile Range on the 13th and 14th, likely from dry microbursts as the exception for the 13th and 14th. More substantial reports of damage and large hail occurred on the 15th ranging from broken skylights, flooding, accumulated hail, and a tornado. Thankfully, there were no reports of injuries or casualties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail broke three skylights, damage is an estimate. Half-dollar size hail reported alongside larger but shorter duration 2.75 inch hail. Hail persisted until approximately 5:10 pm MDT before quitting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low off the coast of California helped funnel in subtropical moisture into the state resulting in the development of widespread showers and storms across central and eastern New Mexico June 5th and 6th. Reports of severe weather and flash flooding stretched from the Rio Grande Valley across portions of eastern NM on June 5th. These reports ranged from a landspout tornado near the Petroglyph National Monument and Nara Visa, NM to flash flooding. Another round of severe weather began in the afternoon of June 6th producing more widespread reports over the same area of the state. Reports ranged from burn scar flash flooding within the Hermits Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar and Santa Rosa, to large hail over parts of San Miguel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire Dept. in Cabo Lucero relayed secondary reports of quarter size hail and accumulated hail 4 to 6 inches deep. Location estimated to be at Mineral Hill based on radar. There were secondary reports of hail damage knocking out windows as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong portion of the jetstream associated with a deep upper level low brought damaging winds to portions of northern and central NM February 22, 2023. Peak gusts regularly topped 70 mph, with the Santa Fe Airport recording a peak gust of 85 mph. The Magdalena Observatory also measured a peak gust of 93 mph in the San Mateo Mountains of western Socorro County. There were numerous reports of down trees, mostly within the Rio Grande Valley stretching from near Socorro to the Albuquerque metro. The most extreme of these wind damage reports included down transmission or communication towers at the Sandia Crest. This storm system also brought winter storm conditions, mostly to western and northern NM. These include reports of heavy snow, road closures along Interstate 40, and an avalanche at the Taos Ski Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sulf WSMR station measured a peak wind gust of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two sequential winter storm systems rolled through northern and central New Mexico on February 13, 2023 and February 14, 2023. Heavy snow was reported across much of the northern mountains of New Mexico, as well as a few isolated spots in and around the Santa Fe and Albuquerque metro areas by the morning of February 14, 2023. Snow accumulations reaching 10 to 11 inches were reported atop the Sangre de Cristo Mountains, while severe winter driving conditions were reported on U.S. Highway 64 in the Tusas Mountains. Strong westerly winds filled in quickly as the main upper level low exited the area during the morning of February 14, 2023. High winds with peak gusts of 60 to 70 mph were reported through the southwest mountains, Sandia and Manzano Mountains, and over a large area of the eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station measured a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mountain wave activity brought another round of high winds impacting parts of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. These winds crashed lee of the mountains along portions of the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains to the I-25 corridor at Raton during the night of February 19, 2023 to the morning of February 20, 2023. Strong westerly winds mixed down to the surface and spread across parts of the eastern plains, impacting parts of Guadalupe and Curry Counties during the afternoon of February 20, 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NMCC mesonet station southeast of Vaughn in southwestern Guadalupe County measured a wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly to southwesterly winds ahead of an approaching upper low impacted portions of central and eastern NM. Peak gusts of 58 to 64 mph were observed from the northern Sangre de Cristo Mountains over to Union County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 58 mph was observed at the Las Vegas Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally cold upper level winter storm system impacted northern and central NM on February 15, 2023. Widespread heavy snow was reported across western, northern, and central NM. Impacts began late in the day on February 14, 2023, peaking in intensity during the morning of February 15, 2023 before the storm exited late in the day. Snow accumulations of over a foot up to 15 inches were reported along the Continental Divide and northern mountain peaks. Blowing snow reducing visibility and snow packed roads caused reports of severe winter travel and forced some roads to be closed. These included I-25 at Raton Pass and U.S. Highway 64 from Raton to Clayton. Strong winds were also present with this storm system, although less pronounced compared to the prior storm system. A lone high wind report at the Roswell Airport was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roswell Industrial Airport ASOS reported a peak gust of 59 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions persisted through February 2023.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong portion of the jetstream associated with a deep upper level low brought damaging winds to portions of northern and central NM February 22, 2023. Peak gusts regularly topped 70 mph, with the Santa Fe Airport recording a peak gust of 85 mph. The Magdalena Observatory also measured a peak gust of 93 mph in the San Mateo Mountains of western Socorro County. There were numerous reports of down trees, mostly within the Rio Grande Valley stretching from near Socorro to the Albuquerque metro. The most extreme of these wind damage reports included down transmission or communication towers at the Sandia Crest. This storm system also brought winter storm conditions, mostly to western and northern NM. These include reports of heavy snow, road closures along Interstate 40, and an avalanche at the Taos Ski Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Phillips Hill WSMR station measured a peak gust of 77 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally cold upper level winter storm system impacted northern and central NM on February 15, 2023. Widespread heavy snow was reported across western, northern, and central NM. Impacts began late in the day on February 14, 2023, peaking in intensity during the morning of February 15, 2023 before the storm exited late in the day. Snow accumulations of over a foot up to 15 inches were reported along the Continental Divide and northern mountain peaks. Blowing snow reducing visibility and snow packed roads caused reports of severe winter travel and forced some roads to be closed. These included I-25 at Raton Pass and U.S. Highway 64 from Raton to Clayton. Strong winds were also present with this storm system, although less pronounced compared to the prior storm system. A lone high wind report at the Roswell Airport was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow impacted the eastern edge of the zone near Taos where there was a nearby report of 8 inches in Valdez. There were various other snow reports of 2 to 5 inches of snow from Taos to Questa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of a strong Pacific storm system brought a strong portion of the jetstream over New Mexico on January 11, 2023. High winds impacted areas along and east of the central mountain chain early in the morning, spreading to most of the eastern plains through the afternoon period before diminishing during the evening hours. Peak gusts regularly ranged from 60 to 68 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station measured a gust of 61 mph.

The second in a series of Pacific storm systems brought winter impacts to western and northern NM on January 17, 2023. This was mostly in the form of heavy snow accumulations with the highest amounts of a 11 to 14.5 inches in the Jemez Mountains. Accumulations of 10 to 12 inches also fell in the Tusas Mountains and Sangre de Cristo Mountains. Winter impacts lasted into the morning of January 18, 2023. High winds also impacted portions of the eastern plains on January 18, 2023 after this storm system exited the state to the east. Strong northwesterly wind gusts peaked to 67 mph at Guadalupe County and De Baca County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS station measured 10 inches of snowfall near Tierra Amarilla.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low crossed northern New Mexico on January 20, 2023 and exited over the Great Plains on January 21, 2023. Heavy snow was observed at numerous locations resulting in several road closures, including on Interstates 25 and 40. Weather related car crashes also resulted, including a flipped snow plow on NM Highway 4 just west of Los Alamos. Strong northerly winds behind the upper low also brought reports of blowing snow through far northeastern NM on January 21, 2023 from Raton to Clayton. This resulted in very low visibility and resulted in the closure of U.S. Highway 64 at Clayton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A twitter reported showed a snow plow having flipped over along New Mexico Highway 4 just west of Los Alamos on the switchbacks to Valles Caldera.

CZ_NAME: MIDDLE RIO GRANDE VALLEY/ALBUQUERQUE METRO AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low dove south over AZ before turning eastward over NM on January 23, 2023. A strong influx of southeasterly to easterly winds filled into eastern NM, funneling between the gaps of the Sandia and Manzano Mountains producing strong east canyon winds at Albuquerque. High winds were recorded through much of the day at the Albuquerque Sunport with a peak gust of 64 mph. The main upper level low also brought heavy snow to portions of the central highlands and eastern plains south of I-40. Mesoscale bands of heavy snow produced localized amounts of 6 to 8 inches over the eastern plains stretching from Vaughn to Clovis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Albuquerque Sunport measured repeated high wind gusts with a peak gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was moving through the Desert Southwest with a 150 knot jet on the back side.  As the trough moved through Southern New Mexico, west to southwest winds gusted up to 76 mph on the White Sands Missile Range with numerous other reports of at least 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet site at the White Sands Missile Range Museum recorded a peak gust of 76 mph.  Other gusts included 75 mph at the White Sands Missile Range Building 1830 and 70 mph at the Main post, San Augustin Pass and Globe mesonet sites. Several car windows on the base also sustained broken windows due to the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong portion of the jetstream associated with a deep upper level low brought damaging winds to portions of northern and central NM February 22, 2023. Peak gusts regularly topped 70 mph, with the Santa Fe Airport recording a peak gust of 85 mph. The Magdalena Observatory also measured a peak gust of 93 mph in the San Mateo Mountains of western Socorro County. There were numerous reports of down trees, mostly within the Rio Grande Valley stretching from near Socorro to the Albuquerque metro. The most extreme of these wind damage reports included down transmission or communication towers at the Sandia Crest. This storm system also brought winter storm conditions, mostly to western and northern NM. These include reports of heavy snow, road closures along Interstate 40, and an avalanche at the Taos Ski Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained storm spotter reported an uprooted and downed tree at San Acacia in north-central Socorro County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly winds in the wake of a departing upper low brought a few rogue gusts up to 58 mph through southwestern Guadalupe County on January 3rd. These winds turned northwesterly with mountain wave activity along the Sangre de Cristo Mountains producing more rogue wind gusts up to 61 mph at Las Vegas and 66 mph southeast of Vaughn on January 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station southeast of Vaughn in southwestern Guadalupe County measured a wind gust of 58 mph.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strengthening westerly winds aloft produced on and off again high wind reports at Angel Fire Airport through the early morning of January 10, 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Angel Fire Airport reported a high sustained wind speed of 43 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of a strong Pacific storm system brought a strong portion of the jetstream over New Mexico on January 11, 2023. High winds impacted areas along and east of the central mountain chain early in the morning, spreading to most of the eastern plains through the afternoon period before diminishing during the evening hours. Peak gusts regularly ranged from 60 to 68 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Taos Airport measured repeated high sustained winds of 40 to 43 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally cold upper level winter storm system impacted northern and central NM on February 15, 2023. Widespread heavy snow was reported across western, northern, and central NM. Impacts began late in the day on February 14, 2023, peaking in intensity during the morning of February 15, 2023 before the storm exited late in the day. Snow accumulations of over a foot up to 15 inches were reported along the Continental Divide and northern mountain peaks. Blowing snow reducing visibility and snow packed roads caused reports of severe winter travel and forced some roads to be closed. These included I-25 at Raton Pass and U.S. Highway 64 from Raton to Clayton. Strong winds were also present with this storm system, although less pronounced compared to the prior storm system. A lone high wind report at the Roswell Airport was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow impacted areas along the Continental Divide where the emergency manager relayed reports from teams clearing areas of 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low crossed northern New Mexico on January 20, 2023 and exited over the Great Plains on January 21, 2023. Heavy snow was observed at numerous locations resulting in several road closures, including on Interstates 25 and 40. Weather related car crashes also resulted, including a flipped snow plow on NM Highway 4 just west of Los Alamos. Strong northerly winds behind the upper low also brought reports of blowing snow through far northeastern NM on January 21, 2023 from Raton to Clayton. This resulted in very low visibility and resulted in the closure of U.S. Highway 64 at Clayton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The New Mexico DOT reported two weather related crashes along I-40 near Clines Corners in the Central Highlands. These crashes resulted in the closure of I-40.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SACRAMENTO MOUNTAINS BELOW 7500 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep cutoff upper low was moving along the International Border with a deep easterly flow over south-central New Mexico.  The deep easterly flow tapped enough moisture to bring close to 6 inches of snow to the eastern slopes of the Sacramento Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 5.8 inches of snow fell 4 miles south-southeast of Mayhill.  Another observer reported 5.1 inches of snow 3 miles west-northwest of Mayhill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly winds mixed down across much northern and central NM on February 28, 2023. A surface low over eastern CO help enhance these wind speeds over far northeastern NM where a high wind gust report was observed at the Clayton Municipal Airport. High sustained wind speeds reaching above 40 mph and peak gusts above 60 mph were observed for an hour or so around noon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clayton Airport measured a high sustained wind speed of 41 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: January 2023 was wetter than normal across a good portion of western NM, whereas drier than normal to record low precipitation was observed across a large swath of the eastern plains. Very little change in drought conditions resulted from this. By months end, roughly 18 percent of the state was in severe to extreme drought, of which about 3 percent was extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small area of severe drought conditions from October 2022 persisted around the Four Corners through January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low moved out of the Great Basin and toward the Four Corners region.  A surface cold front also pushed through southern New Mexico ahead of the upper low bringing heavy precipitation to the Gila Region with rain changing over to snow. Over 6 inches of snow was reported around Silver City with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall amount reported was 6.3 inches 4.6 miles west of Silver City.  Other reports included 6.0 inches 4.4 miles north of Silver City, 5.5 inches 3.4 miles southwest of Silver city and 4 inches about 9 miles east-northeast of Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and broad upper-level trough was located across the western US. A very strong jet stream with numerous embedded shortwaves resulted in westerly strong and high winds across much of the region. Intense high winds were reported across the higher elevations with high winds and blowing dust across a large portion of the plains.

This event consisted of a highly amplified weather pattern with an ejecting closed upper-low moving into the southern Rocky Mountains. This system and accompanying jet brought a high-end wind event to southeast New Mexico and much of west Texas. High winds and property damage were reported across a large portion of the area. Additionally, blowing dust greatly reduced visibility at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening upper level low pressure system tracked west to east from the Pacific over northern New Mexico on January 1st and 2nd. The storm brought healthy amounts of snow mostly to western and northern New Mexico. There were isolated winter storm conditions that accompanied this system with a 10 inch snow report in the Jemez Mountains and an 11 inch snow report in the Tusas Mountains of northern New Mexico. Difficult winter travel conditions were also reported through the northern mountains of New Mexico from snow packed and icy roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow impacted the area with the Hopewell SNOTEL reporting an estimated 11 inches of snowfall. Difficult driving conditions were also reported on area highways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of Pacific storm systems brought winter impacts to western and northern NM on January 17, 2023. This was mostly in the form of heavy snow accumulations with the highest amounts of a 11 to 14.5 inches in the Jemez Mountains. Accumulations of 10 to 12 inches also fell in the Tusas Mountains and Sangre de Cristo Mountains. Winter impacts lasted into the morning of January 18, 2023. High winds also impacted portions of the eastern plains on January 18, 2023 after this storm system exited the state to the east. Strong northwesterly wind gusts peaked to 67 mph at Guadalupe County and De Baca County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow impacted the area. A CoCoRaHS observer measured 14.5 inches of snowfall along the western caldera of the Jemez Mountains.

CZ_NAME: SAN AGUSTIN PLAINS AND ADJACENT LOWLANDS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low dove south over AZ before turning eastward over NM on January 23, 2023. A strong influx of southeasterly to easterly winds filled into eastern NM, funneling between the gaps of the Sandia and Manzano Mountains producing strong east canyon winds at Albuquerque. High winds were recorded through much of the day at the Albuquerque Sunport with a peak gust of 64 mph. The main upper level low also brought heavy snow to portions of the central highlands and eastern plains south of I-40. Mesoscale bands of heavy snow produced localized amounts of 6 to 8 inches over the eastern plains stretching from Vaughn to Clovis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow impacted portions of the zone near Magdalena where a CoCoRaHS observer reported 4.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally cold upper level winter storm system impacted northern and central NM on February 15, 2023. Widespread heavy snow was reported across western, northern, and central NM. Impacts began late in the day on February 14, 2023, peaking in intensity during the morning of February 15, 2023 before the storm exited late in the day. Snow accumulations of over a foot up to 15 inches were reported along the Continental Divide and northern mountain peaks. Blowing snow reducing visibility and snow packed roads caused reports of severe winter travel and forced some roads to be closed. These included I-25 at Raton Pass and U.S. Highway 64 from Raton to Clayton. Strong winds were also present with this storm system, although less pronounced compared to the prior storm system. A lone high wind report at the Roswell Airport was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: I-25 was closed at Raton Pass with severe winter driving conditions from snow packed and icy roads and blowing snow with the closure and winter conditions stretching south through the town of Raton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow rounding the northeastern periphery of an upper high over AZ combined with abundant low-level moisture over the high plains of eastern NM to produce severe weather along and east of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on July 21st and 22nd. Large hail and severe winds were observed from these scattered storm cells. Heavy rainfall also produced flash flooding on the Hermit's Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar on July 21st. Fast flowing flood waters with burn scar debris was observed at several locations causing damage to properties and forcing two road closures north and south of Mora, NM. Flash flooding was also reported near the Ute Park burn scar along the Ponil Creek.

Higher low-level moisture on July 13th and a backdoor cold front replenishing this moisture for July 14th and 15th combined with robust upper level wind shear produced three days of severe weather across eastern NM. Severe weather impacts were limited to northeastern NM on the 13th and 14th, becoming more widespread across northeastern and east-central NM on the 15th. There were a few thunderstorm wind gusts measured in the vicinity of the White Sands Missile Range on the 13th and 14th, likely from dry microbursts as the exception for the 13th and 14th. More substantial reports of damage and large hail occurred on the 15th ranging from broken skylights, flooding, accumulated hail, and a tornado. Thankfully, there were no reports of injuries or casualties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong portion of the jetstream associated with a deep upper level low brought damaging winds to portions of northern and central NM February 22, 2023. Peak gusts regularly topped 70 mph, with the Santa Fe Airport recording a peak gust of 85 mph. The Magdalena Observatory also measured a peak gust of 93 mph in the San Mateo Mountains of western Socorro County. There were numerous reports of down trees, mostly within the Rio Grande Valley stretching from near Socorro to the Albuquerque metro. The most extreme of these wind damage reports included down transmission or communication towers at the Sandia Crest. This storm system also brought winter storm conditions, mostly to western and northern NM. These include reports of heavy snow, road closures along Interstate 40, and an avalanche at the Taos Ski Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Albuquerque Sunport ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 73 mph.||A trained storm spotter reported wind damage of uprooted and downed trees taking down a metal fence in front of St. Joseph's High School at Coors and St. Josephs Drive.

There were several significant heavy rain events that occurred over parts of eastern NM during the month of May. These events, while causing flooding impacts, also improved drought conditions across a wide swath of eastern NM. This resulted in the complete removal of extreme and exceptional drought conditions with the overall coverage of severe drought conditions also lowering some.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved in Curry County during the month of May with the removal of extreme drought conditions. A small area of severe drought conditions persisted in the southern section of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several consecutive days of significant severe weather impacted eastern New Mexico from May 24-26, 2023. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the dryline and east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on May 23rd. Storms tracked east and southeast across the eastern plains of New Mexico. Extensive severe weather was reported when these storms encountered greater moisture and lift closer to the Texas border. Hail up to 3.5 inches in diameter, thunderstorm wind gusts of 80 to 90 mph, and three tornados were reported in an area stretching from Union County to Roosevelt County. The largest hail stones and strongest wind gusts focused throughout Curry County during the evening hours. Hail and wind damage was reported in Tucumcari, Grady, and Clovis. Three separate tornadoes were reported in northern Curry County. Thunderstorms also dropped very heavy rainfall of 2 to 4 inches of rain across portions of Quay, De Baca, Curry, and Roosevelt Counties. Urban flooding occurred in Clovis, NM, causing some roads to be closed, and some cars to be stranded. Severe weather continued through the night and into May 25th with numerous reports of large hail, strong winds, and flash flooding. Quay County was particularly hard hit with baseball size hail and torrential rainfall amounts in excess of 5 inches. Significant hail damage was reported across Tucumcari with widespread flooding across the county. Severe wind gusts of 70 to 80 mph were common with these storms with damage reported. Strong to severe storms continued well into the night once again on the 25th with more heavy rainfall around the Caprock. The focus for severe weather shifted farther west on May 26th with large hail and heavy rainfall reported around Torrance County. A tornado and baseball size hail was also reported with a supercell near Encino. This activity spread eastward through the day with large hail, damaging wind gusts, and more torrential rainfall around the Caprock well into the night of May 26th. Three-day rainfall amounts exceeded 6 inches in many parts of Curry and Quay counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports from storm chasers of large hail ranging in size from 2 to 3.5 inches just east-northeast of Tucumcari along U.S. Highway 54. Thankfully there were no reports of damage directly related to the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher low-level moisture on July 13th and a backdoor cold front replenishing this moisture for July 14th and 15th combined with robust upper level wind shear produced three days of severe weather across eastern NM. Severe weather impacts were limited to northeastern NM on the 13th and 14th, becoming more widespread across northeastern and east-central NM on the 15th. There were a few thunderstorm wind gusts measured in the vicinity of the White Sands Missile Range on the 13th and 14th, likely from dry microbursts as the exception for the 13th and 14th. More substantial reports of damage and large hail occurred on the 15th ranging from broken skylights, flooding, accumulated hail, and a tornado. Thankfully, there were no reports of injuries or casualties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station within the White Sands Missile Range measured a 65 mph thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of afternoon convection developed over the northern mountains of NM. One cell produced a funnel cloud while strengthening over U.S. Highway 84 near Cebolla, NM. No damage or touchdown was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter shared a photo of a funnel cloud that formed along U.S. Highway 84 near Cebolla.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong portion of the jetstream associated with a deep upper level low brought damaging winds to portions of northern and central NM February 22, 2023. Peak gusts regularly topped 70 mph, with the Santa Fe Airport recording a peak gust of 85 mph. The Magdalena Observatory also measured a peak gust of 93 mph in the San Mateo Mountains of western Socorro County. There were numerous reports of down trees, mostly within the Rio Grande Valley stretching from near Socorro to the Albuquerque metro. The most extreme of these wind damage reports included down transmission or communication towers at the Sandia Crest. This storm system also brought winter storm conditions, mostly to western and northern NM. These include reports of heavy snow, road closures along Interstate 40, and an avalanche at the Taos Ski Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glorieta CWOP measured a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry surface conditions combined with an influx of mid-level moisture allowed for scattered virga showers and dry thunderstorms to develop over central and eastern NM during the afternoon hours of June 27th. Thankfully, there were no reports of damages or injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fort Sumner Airport reported a thunderstorm wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow rounding the northeastern periphery of an upper high over AZ combined with abundant low-level moisture over the high plains of eastern NM to produce severe weather along and east of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on July 21st and 22nd. Large hail and severe winds were observed from these scattered storm cells. Heavy rainfall also produced flash flooding on the Hermit's Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar on July 21st. Fast flowing flood waters with burn scar debris was observed at several locations causing damage to properties and forcing two road closures north and south of Mora, NM. Flash flooding was also reported near the Ute Park burn scar along the Ponil Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media shared a video on social media from a viewer showing flash flooding. Fast flowing water with hail and debris was flowing all across the property and beneath the home. Damage is an estimate. There were no reports of injuries or casualties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong segment of the jetstream associated with a larger upper level storm system passed over NM April 3, 2023. Strong southwesterly winds were associated with this storm system and reached the portions of west-central, northwestern, the northern mountains and adjacent highlands during the 3rd. A cold front passed west to east across NM that night into the morning of April 4, 2023 bringing a westerly and northwesterly wind shift. More widespread high winds resulted behind this system as it exited into the Great Plains on the 4th. High wind reports were observed across much of southwestern, central, and eastern sections of the state. Despite there being many reports of 60 to 70 mph wind gusts, there were no shared reports of damage. A couple of small fires started in the high winds, but no damage was reported from these.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public weather station at Sumner Lake measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system accompanied by a strong portion of the jetstream brought high winds to portions of central and eastern NM. These southwest winds reached nearly the entire state with high wind gusts being reported from Santa Fe to Albuquerque and through the highlands from Las Vegas to Clines Corners and Vaughn. Areas of blowing dust resulted from these winds, slightly dropping visibilities. The high winds also aided in the rapid spread of a new wildfire near Sapello, NM during the afternoon. There were no reports of damage or injuries directly related to the high winds. Meanwhile, severe weather broke out across far eastern NM along the TX border during the afternoon and evening hours. Thunderstorm activity favored a dryline feature with individual storm cells producing mostly pea to nickel size hail and severe wind gusts. Several severe wind gusts were reported in portions of northeastern NM with one report of one inch size hail near Clovis. Nickel size hail was also reported in San Jon, NM. There were no reports of wind or hail damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raton Airport ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 68 mph from a thunderstorm gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unnamed brush fire was reported in eastern Clovis, started by sparks coming off a dragging hydraulic plate on a fire truck transporting an aerial unit into town. In fact multiple fires were started as the truck continued down State Highway 523. The brush fires quickly grew due to southerly to southwesterly winds gusting 25 to 35 mph with very low humidity. The fires threatened multiple structures including horse stables, barns, and homes. Thankfully only three unoccupied outbuildings were damaged. These structures were demolished after the fire was brought under control late that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple brush fires caused by sparks from a fire truck grew out of control in eastern Clovis. Three unoccupied outbuildings were damaged by the fire, demolished after the fire was brought under control. No injuries or casualties were reported. Damage cost based on the unoccupied buildings being demolished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A typical Spring southwesterly high wind event occurred April 19, 2023 with several locations observing high wind gusts of 58 mph or higher. A strong section of the jetstream associated with an upper level storm system crossed NM. Peak wind gusts ranging from 58 to 66 mph were observed at sporadic locations across northern and central NM during the afternoon hours. There were even two reports of blown over semi trucks along highways, one south of Shiprock and the other just north of Los Lunas. Thankfully there were no shared reports of injuries or fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Navajo Nation PD reported to KOAT, a semi truck was blown over due to high winds along State Road 491 south of Shiprock. Damages and injuries are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms developed over the high plains of east-central and southeastern NM during the afternoon of May 19, 2023. One of these storms passed over the Lake Arthur area south of Roswell in south-central Chaves County. This storm produced golf ball size hail and a peak thunderstorm wind gust of 62 mph. There were no reports of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported nickel to golf ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent period of above normal moisture produced another day of scattered to numerous thunderstorms over northern and central NM. A few of these storms produced large hail and locally heavy rainfall. A report of quarter size hail was received from Socorro, NM producing no damage. Locally heavy rainfall caused flash flooding in Farmington, NM. Emergency management reported many streets underwater with water marks up to businesses. Thankfully it stopped there and there was no report of property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county emergency manager estimated quarter size hail in Socorro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist, backdoor cold front that moved west through the Rio Grande Valley early on May 21, 2023 provided lift for afternoon showers and thunderstorms. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail reported near the Rio Grande Nature Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon dry thunderstorms and virga showers spread across a good portion of northern and central NM on April 25, 2023 ahead of an approaching storm system. A few of these produced localized dry microbursts that impacted isolated spots. Several peak gusts of 58 to 66 mph were observed. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Santa Fe Airport ASOS measured a peak gust of 58 mph from a localized dry microburst. No damage was reported.

The end of monsoon season for New Mexico saw continued near to below normal precipitation over much of the area. As a result, extreme drought expanded across sections of northwestern NM and into portions of the middle Rio Grande Valley that include the Albuquerque metro area. Exceptional drought developed over parts of Chaves County near Roswell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of a dry monsoon season and above normal temperatures allowed for the expansion of extreme drought conditions in the southeastern portion of the zone.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe and extreme drought persisted through April 2023 and even expanded slightly to cover the far northern section of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher low-level moisture on July 13th and a backdoor cold front replenishing this moisture for July 14th and 15th combined with robust upper level wind shear produced three days of severe weather across eastern NM. Severe weather impacts were limited to northeastern NM on the 13th and 14th, becoming more widespread across northeastern and east-central NM on the 15th. There were a few thunderstorm wind gusts measured in the vicinity of the White Sands Missile Range on the 13th and 14th, likely from dry microbursts as the exception for the 13th and 14th. More substantial reports of damage and large hail occurred on the 15th ranging from broken skylights, flooding, accumulated hail, and a tornado. Thankfully, there were no reports of injuries or casualties.

Abundant monsoon moisture allowed for afternoon thunderstorms to develop over the central mountain chain, including the Sacramento Mountains near Ruidoso. Little to no steering flow allowed for storms to reside over the same location for a long period of time producing locally heavy rainfall. One such incident resulted in flash flooding along the upper Rio Ruidoso just west of Ruidoso near the Lincoln and Otero County line. Floodwaters carried mud, rocks, and debris across Mescalero Lane. Photos shared show what appears to be only minor damage to an access gate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Floodwaters carried significant amounts of mud, rocks, and debris across Mescalero Lane along the Rio Ruidoso west of Ruidoso. There was minor damage to an access gate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of monsoon season for New Mexico saw continued near to below normal precipitation over much of the area. As a result, extreme drought expanded across sections of northwestern NM and into portions of the middle Rio Grande Valley that include the Albuquerque metro area. Exceptional drought developed over parts of Chaves County near Roswell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of a dry monsoon season and above normal temperatures allowed for the persistence of severe drought conditions into southern and far northeastern portions of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system accompanied by a strong portion of the jetstream brought high winds to portions of central and eastern NM. These southwest winds reached nearly the entire state with high wind gusts being reported from Santa Fe to Albuquerque and through the highlands from Las Vegas to Clines Corners and Vaughn. Areas of blowing dust resulted from these winds, slightly dropping visibilities. The high winds also aided in the rapid spread of a new wildfire near Sapello, NM during the afternoon. There were no reports of damage or injuries directly related to the high winds. Meanwhile, severe weather broke out across far eastern NM along the TX border during the afternoon and evening hours. Thunderstorm activity favored a dryline feature with individual storm cells producing mostly pea to nickel size hail and severe wind gusts. Several severe wind gusts were reported in portions of northeastern NM with one report of one inch size hail near Clovis. Nickel size hail was also reported in San Jon, NM. There were no reports of wind or hail damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tucumcari Airport ASOS measured a peak thunderstorm wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong segment of the jetstream associated with a larger upper level storm system passed over NM April 3, 2023. Strong southwesterly winds were associated with this storm system and reached the portions of west-central, northwestern, the northern mountains and adjacent highlands during the 3rd. A cold front passed west to east across NM that night into the morning of April 4, 2023 bringing a westerly and northwesterly wind shift. More widespread high winds resulted behind this system as it exited into the Great Plains on the 4th. High wind reports were observed across much of southwestern, central, and eastern sections of the state. Despite there being many reports of 60 to 70 mph wind gusts, there were no shared reports of damage. A couple of small fires started in the high winds, but no damage was reported from these.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Taos Ski Valley measured a peak wind gust of 81 mph at Kachina Peak. This station regularly saw wind gusts over 60 mph during April 3rd and 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal snowpack from the prior winter season in combination with a rapid warming in temperatures resulted in a rapid snowmelt within the Jemez Mountains and the Jemez River Basin. On April 12, 2023, the increase in flow approached the Minor flood stage of 8.0 feet at 7.67 feet. Despite not reaching Minor flood stage, the increased flow resulted in the Jemez Springs Wastewater Treatment Plant to overflow into the Jemez River. River levels also crossed a few low water crossings one mile south of Jemez Pueblo toward San Ysidro, and two vehicle water rescues were needed on April 12, 2023. The river reached a peak flow of 7.85 feet early on the morning of April 13, 2023 at 9:15 MDT before receding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local officials reported that the rapid rise in river levels from rapid snowmelt on the Jemez River caused the Jemez Springs Wastewater Treatment Facility to overflow. Two vehicle water rescues were also needed one mile south of Jemez Pueblo due to high river levels crossing the local highway. The flooding occurred despite the river levels cresting at 7.67 feet, which is below the minor flood stage of 8.00 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mountain wave activity brought another round of high winds impacting parts of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. These winds crashed lee of the mountains along portions of the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains to the I-25 corridor at Raton during the night of February 19, 2023 to the morning of February 20, 2023. Strong westerly winds mixed down to the surface and spread across parts of the eastern plains, impacting parts of Guadalupe and Curry Counties during the afternoon of February 20, 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raton Municipal Airport measured a gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed over the northeastern highlands of NM between Las Vegas and Raton on the afternoon of April 12, 2023. Due to dry surface conditions, downdrafts from these storms produced strong wind gusts. One was observed to hit the ASOS at the Raton Municipal Airport, reaching severe strength at 59 mph. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raton Crews Airport ASOS measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A typical Spring southwesterly high wind event occurred April 19, 2023 with several locations observing high wind gusts of 58 mph or higher. A strong section of the jetstream associated with an upper level storm system crossed NM. Peak wind gusts ranging from 58 to 66 mph were observed at sporadic locations across northern and central NM during the afternoon hours. There were even two reports of blown over semi trucks along highways, one south of Shiprock and the other just north of Los Lunas. Thankfully there were no shared reports of injuries or fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gallup Airport ASOS measured a peak gust of 58 mph.

Dry and windy conditions produced localized critical fire weather conditions within the Estancia Valley on April 2, 2023. Winds were strong enough to allow for the Echo Ridge wildfire to undergo rapid fire spread. Unfortunately three homes and seven outbuildings were destroyed, but no casualties and one injury to a fire fighter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Echo Ridge fire started as a grassfire and quickly spread south of Moriarty to the Echo Ridge community. The Torrance County emergency manager requested the National Weather Service to issue a fire warning for people within the community to evacuate. Unfortunately the fire still destroyed three homes and seven outbuildings according to media reports the next day. The fire burned 175 acres, and was reported to be mostly contained by the afternoon of April 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow rounding the northeastern periphery of an upper high over AZ combined with abundant low-level moisture over the high plains of eastern NM to produce severe weather along and east of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on July 21st and 22nd. Large hail and severe winds were observed from these scattered storm cells. Heavy rainfall also produced flash flooding on the Hermit's Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar on July 21st. Fast flowing flood waters with burn scar debris was observed at several locations causing damage to properties and forcing two road closures north and south of Mora, NM. Flash flooding was also reported near the Ute Park burn scar along the Ponil Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Melrose Gunnery weather station measured a peak thunderstorm wind gust of 69 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of July 24th, a decaying thunderstorm produced a microburst wind gust that caused damage to a residential solar array just southwest of Moriarty.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm downburst lifted a large solar panel unit and its concrete pillars out of the ground and tossed it several feet away. Many of the panels and the steel frame were damaged or destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning storms drifting northward into southern Socorro County near the Oscura Mountains produced heavy rainfall. This resulted in water running over WSMR Range Road 7 that was two to three feet deep. Mud and debris was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several flood areas along White Sands Missile Range Road Seven. Water was two to three feet deep with mud and debris on the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher moisture across southern New Mexico along with lift from a subtle disturbance resulted in the development of scattered showers and storms across south central and southeast New Mexico. Some storms produced nickel sized hail in southern Socorro County and at Corona in Lincoln County.

Very dry surface conditions combined with an influx of mid-level moisture allowed for scattered virga showers and dry thunderstorms to develop over central and eastern NM during the afternoon hours of June 27th. Several of these produced severe downburst winds that impacted sporadic locations across eastern NM. These included Fort Sumner, Tucumcari, Estancia, Las Vegas, Carrizozo,  and Raton. Thankfully, there were no reports of damages or injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raton Airport ASOS measured a 64 mph then 81 mph thunderstorm wind gusts within a five minute time span from one outflow boundary.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter size hail that produced damage to windows and skylights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher low-level moisture on July 13th and a backdoor cold front replenishing this moisture for July 14th and 15th combined with robust upper level wind shear produced three days of severe weather across eastern NM. Severe weather impacts were limited to northeastern NM on the 13th and 14th, becoming more widespread across northeastern and east-central NM on the 15th. There were a few thunderstorm wind gusts measured in the vicinity of the White Sands Missile Range on the 13th and 14th, likely from dry microbursts as the exception for the 13th and 14th. More substantial reports of damage and large hail occurred on the 15th ranging from broken skylights, flooding, accumulated hail, and a tornado. Thankfully, there were no reports of injuries or casualties.

CZ_NAME: MIDDLE RIO GRANDE VALLEY/ALBUQUERQUE METRO AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system accompanied by a strong portion of the jetstream brought high winds to portions of central and eastern NM. These southwest winds reached nearly the entire state with high wind gusts being reported from Santa Fe to Albuquerque and through the highlands from Las Vegas to Clines Corners and Vaughn. Areas of blowing dust resulted from these winds, slightly dropping visibilities. The high winds also aided in the rapid spread of a new wildfire near Sapello, NM during the afternoon. There were no reports of damage or injuries directly related to the high winds. Meanwhile, severe weather broke out across far eastern NM along the TX border during the afternoon and evening hours. Thunderstorm activity favored a dryline feature with individual storm cells producing mostly pea to nickel size hail and severe wind gusts. Several severe wind gusts were reported in portions of northeastern NM with one report of one inch size hail near Clovis. Nickel size hail was also reported in San Jon, NM. There were no reports of wind or hail damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Albuquerque Airport ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent period of above normal moisture produced another day of scattered to numerous thunderstorms over northern and central NM. A few of these storms produced large hail and locally heavy rainfall. A report of quarter size hail was received from Socorro, NM producing no damage. Locally heavy rainfall caused flash flooding in Farmington, NM. Emergency management reported many streets underwater with water marks up to businesses. Thankfully it stopped there and there was no report of property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported flash flooding with several streets underwater. Photos were received of street flooding at Main Street and Orchard Avenue. High water marks were up to the front doors of businesses with some using sandbags. There were no reports of property damage or casualties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed along a sharpening dryline across eastern NM. Storms strengthened as they tracked eastward toward the Texas border, producing large hail and even a landspout tornado. Hail reports ranged in size from pennies to golf balls and were focused across Quay and Curry counties. The landspout was reported just south of Texico and caused no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained storm spotter reported quarter size hail 5 miles north of Pleasant Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The overall coverage of showers and thunderstorms with severe hail, high winds, and flash flooding began decreasing over eastern NM as a much larger area of dry air spread over the state on May 27, 2023. The night came to an end with one last report of quarter size hail around Raton. No damage was reported with these large hail reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed along a sharpening dryline across eastern NM. Storms strengthened as they tracked eastward toward the Texas border, producing large hail and even a landspout tornado. Hail reports ranged in size from pennies to golf balls and were focused across Quay and Curry counties. The landspout was reported just south of Texico and caused no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a public report of golf ball size hail in Broadview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The overall coverage of showers and thunderstorms with severe hail, high winds, and flash flooding began decreasing over eastern NM as a much larger area of dry air spread over the state on May 27, 2023. A couple strong to severe thunderstorms moved east from Guadalupe and De Baca counties onto the Caprock region of Curry, Quay, and Roosevelt counties by the late evening hours of the 27th. Quarter to golf ball size hail and locally heavy rainfall was reported with a couple of these storms around Fort Sumner. The night came to an end with one last report of quarter size hail around Raton. No damage was reported with these large hail reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system accompanied by a strong portion of the jetstream brought high winds to portions of central and eastern NM. These southwest winds reached nearly the entire state with high wind gusts being reported from Santa Fe to Albuquerque and through the highlands from Las Vegas to Clines Corners and Vaughn. Areas of blowing dust resulted from these winds, slightly dropping visibilities. The high winds also aided in the rapid spread of a new wildfire near Sapello, NM during the afternoon. There were no reports of damage or injuries directly related to the high winds. Meanwhile, severe weather broke out across far eastern NM along the TX border during the afternoon and evening hours. Thunderstorm activity favored a dryline feature with individual storm cells producing mostly pea to nickel size hail and severe wind gusts. Several severe wind gusts were reported in portions of northeastern NM with one report of one inch size hail near Clovis. Nickel size hail was also reported in San Jon, NM. There were no reports of wind or hail damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Santa Fe Airport ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms developed over the high plains of east-central and southeastern NM during the afternoon of May 19, 2023. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough was moving across the Great Basin with strong southwest flow, including a 110 knot jet, across southern New Mexico. Winds across the region gusted up to 74 mph at San Augustin Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 74 mph was recorded at San Augustin Pass. Other gusts included 59 mph at the White Sands Missile Range Main Post and 58 mph at Condron Field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low was moving near the International Border with a 120 knot jet ahead of it.  Strong winds ahead an approaching cold front gusted up to 79 mph at Salinas Peak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong portion of the jetstream overrode the Sangre de Cristo Mountains, reaching the surface during the afternoon of March 5, 2023 and lasting overnight into the early hours of March 6, 2023. Peak wind gusts of 60 to 71 mph were observed, with the highest gust of 71 being observed in the northern Sangre de Cristo Mountains. High wind gusts also reached down to the I-25 corridor between Las Vegas and Raton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public weather station at Kachina Peak measured a peak gust of 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong segment of the jetstream brought high winds over NM on March 11, 2023. These high winds overrode the Sangre de Cristo Mountains reaching down to the adjacent northeastern highlands along I-25 from Las Vegas to Raton, NM. Peak gusts reaching to near or over 60 mph were recorded at these two towns. Strong westerly winds also reaching down to the surface over the east-central plains of NM along the I-40 corridor through Guadalupe and Quay Counties. Peak gusts above 60 mph were recorded by mesonet stations in western Guadalupe County and at the Tucumcari Airport in Quay County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 59 mph was observed at the Raton Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low winter storm system tracked over southern portions of New Mexico. Moderate to heavy snow amounts were observed atop the peaks of the southwest mountains, Jemez Mountains, and Sangre de Cristo Mountains from this event. These totals ranged from 10 to 11.5 inches. Already present snow packed and icy roads from prior events were exacerbated by this winter storm event, keeping hazardous winter travel conditions going for many high elevation mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow impacted a small portion of the zone, where an estimated ten inches of snow fell at the Silver Creek Divide SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system crossing the western U.S. brought a strong portion of the jetstream over NM March 30, 2023. Aided by a deep surface low pressure system over eastern CO, strong winds aloft reached the surface throughout the day. These winds did not entirely die off overnight and turned westerly and then northwesterly on March 31, 2023. Wind speeds restrengthened to high wind speeds again on March 31st focused mostly through east-central and northeastern NM before finally tapering off through the afternoon. Peak wind gusts generally ranged from 60 to 70 mph with a top speed of 86 mph being observed at the Magdalena Ridge Observatory. Wind damage was also reported along I-25 at Tijeras and in Estancia on March 30th. Reduced visibility from isolated blowing dust along I-25 was also reported at Wagon Mound and observed on satellite near Springer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raton Municipal Airport reported a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 16, 2023, a strong backdoor cold front swept from north to south through eastern NM. Strong northerly winds accompanied and followed in the wake of this cold front, especially along the NM and TX border. A high wind gust of 69 mph was reported near Clovis by a mesonet station during the afternoon period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The GW0819 mesonet station near Clovis, NM measured a peak wind gust of 69 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance passed over northern NM late March 16, 2023 and through the morning of March 17, 2023. Preceding moisture associated with this system on the 16th produced heavy snow over the northern mountains or NM. The main disturbance and colder temperatures brought snow levels and bands of snow to lower elevations of west-central and central NM during the morning of March 17, 2023. These areas included the Santa Fe and Albuquerque metro areas. Several crashes caused road closures, including on I-25 and several state highways, resulting from snow producing low visibility. The highest snow accumulations of 1 to 2 feet were observed over the high terrain of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow impacted the western high terrain of the Jemez Mountains where the Vacas Locas SNOTEL measured 11 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system crossing the western U.S. brought a strong portion of the jetstream over NM March 30, 2023. Aided by a deep surface low pressure system over eastern CO, strong winds aloft reached the surface throughout the day. These winds did not entirely die off overnight and turned westerly and then northwesterly on March 31, 2023. Wind speeds restrengthened to high wind speeds again on March 31st focused mostly through east-central and northeastern NM before finally tapering off through the afternoon. Peak wind gusts generally ranged from 60 to 70 mph with a top speed of 86 mph being observed at the Magdalena Ridge Observatory. Wind damage was also reported along I-25 at Tijeras and in Estancia on March 30th. Reduced visibility from isolated blowing dust along I-25 was also reported at Wagon Mound and observed on satellite near Springer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clovis Municipal Airport measured a high sustained wind speed of 44 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought continued to improve across northern and central NM during March, 2023. The area with no drought conditions increased to nearly half of the state. The areas of severe to extreme drought remained the same as the month before, entirely focused through the eastern half of the state. At month's end this included 16 percent in severe drought, of which 4 percent was in extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions improved to moderate drought through a majority of the Northwest Highlands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing southwest winds were observed on March 21, 2023. The strongest winds focused through the south-central high terrain, namely the Sacramento Mountains. A peak wind gust of 58 mph was observed by the Smokey Bear RAWS right on the edge of Ruidoso. The nearby Sierra Blanca airport also reported a peak wind gust of 56 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Smokey Bear RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph. The nearby Sierra Blanca airport also measured a peak wind gust of 56 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong section of the jetstream crossed NM producing strong southwest winds across much of southern, central, and eastern NM on March 22, 2023. High wind gusts regularly reached 60 to 70 mph with a few peak wind gusts reaching near 70 mph or more at the Sandia Crest and Magdalena Ridge. There were surprisingly no reports of wind damage collected, and no reports of reduced visibility from blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sandia Tram at the crest observed a peak wind gust of 72 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong segment of the jetstream brought high winds over NM on March 11, 2023. These high winds overrode the Sangre de Cristo Mountains reaching down to the adjacent northeastern highlands along I-25 from Las Vegas to Raton, NM. Peak gusts reaching to near or over 60 mph were recorded at these two towns. Strong westerly winds also reaching down to the surface over the east-central plains of NM along the I-40 corridor through Guadalupe and Quay Counties. Peak gusts above 60 mph were recorded by mesonet stations in western Guadalupe County and at the Tucumcari Airport in Quay County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 67 mph was observed by the SE Vaughn mesonet station.

These winds did not entirely die off overnight and turned westerly and then northwesterly on March 31, 2023. Wind speeds restrengthened to high wind speeds again on March 31st focused mostly through east-central and northeastern NM before finally tapering off through the afternoon. Peak wind gusts generally ranged from 60 to 70 mph with a top speed of 86 mph being observed at the Magdalena Ridge Observatory. Wind damage was also reported along I-25 at Tijeras and in Estancia on March 30th. Reduced visibility from isolated blowing dust along I-25 was also reported at Wagon Mound and observed on satellite near Springer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station southeast of Vaughn reported a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, deep trough over the Intermountain West brought westerly strong to high wind across a large portion of southeast New Mexico and west Texas.

This is a continuation of the previous day's event. A large, positively-tilted trough over the western US with a leading jet streak resulted in a prolonged and widespread period of high winds across much of southeast New Mexico and west Texas. Wind damage was reported in some locations due to this high-end synoptic wind event.

CZ_NAME: JOHNSON AND BARTLETT MESAS INCLUDING RATON PASS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were several significant heavy rain events that occurred over parts of eastern NM during the month of May. These events, while causing flooding impacts, also improved drought conditions across a wide swath of eastern NM. This resulted in the complete removal of extreme and exceptional drought conditions with the overall coverage of severe drought conditions also lowering some.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from October 2022 persisted through May 2023.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large closed upper-low was located over the northern Great Plains with a shortwave trough to the south within the sub-tropical jet stream. These features helped bring rich moisture and instability into the region. Widespread strong to severe thunderstorms developed across much of the region. The Permian Basin and the Stockton Plateau were the two main areas of note. Severe winds, large hail. and a brief tornado was observed in this northern complex. To the south, several long-track, cyclic supercells produced tornadoes and very large hail over rural portions of Pecos and Terrell Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-size hail observed in downtown Hobbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several consecutive days of significant severe weather impacted eastern New Mexico from May 24-26, 2023. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the dryline and east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on May 23rd. Storms tracked east and southeast across the eastern plains of New Mexico. Extensive severe weather was reported when these storms encountered greater moisture and lift closer to the Texas border. Hail up to 3.5 inches in diameter, thunderstorm wind gusts of 80 to 90 mph, and three tornados were reported in an area stretching from Union County to Roosevelt County. The largest hail stones and strongest wind gusts focused throughout Curry County during the evening hours. Hail and wind damage was reported in Tucumcari, Grady, and Clovis. Three separate tornadoes were reported in northern Curry County. Thunderstorms also dropped very heavy rainfall of 2 to 4 inches of rain across portions of Quay, De Baca, Curry, and Roosevelt Counties. Urban flooding occurred in Clovis, NM, causing some roads to be closed, and some cars to be stranded. Severe weather continued through the night and into May 25th with numerous reports of large hail, strong winds, and flash flooding. Quay County was particularly hard hit with baseball size hail and torrential rainfall amounts in excess of 5 inches. Significant hail damage was reported across Tucumcari with widespread flooding across the county. Severe wind gusts of 70 to 80 mph were common with these storms with damage reported. Strong to severe storms continued well into the night once again on the 25th with more heavy rainfall around the Caprock. The focus for severe weather shifted farther west on May 26th with large hail and heavy rainfall reported around Torrance County. A tornado and baseball size hail was also reported with a supercell near Encino. This activity spread eastward through the day with large hail, damaging wind gusts, and more torrential rainfall around the Caprock well into the night of May 26th. Three-day rainfall amounts exceeded 6 inches in many parts of Curry and Quay counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of monsoon season for New Mexico saw continued near to below normal precipitation over much of the area. As a result, extreme drought expanded across sections of northwestern NM and into portions of the middle Rio Grande Valley that include the Albuquerque metro area. Exceptional drought developed over parts of Chaves County near Roswell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of a dry monsoon season and above normal temperatures allowed for the persistence of severe drought conditions in the southern part of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms developed across the high terrain of western New Mexico during the early afternoon hours. One of these storms moved northeast through Albuquerque producing penny to quarter sized hail in the southwest and central part of the city.

Hot and dry conditions continued during the month of August across northern and central New Mexico. The lack of monsoon showers and storms allowed drought conditions to continue to deteriorate areawide with most of the region under severe to extreme drought, outside of parts of west central New Mexico and most of northeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of a dry monsoon season and above normal temperatures allowed for the introduction of a patch of severe drought conditions in northern portions of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front and shortwave trough across eastern New Mexico helped storms initiate over the central mountain chain during the early afternoon hours. These storms produced rapid flash flooding over the McBride burn scar in Ruidoso. As storms moved east and south off the high terrain, they eventually congealed into a line with strong wind gusts reported at Encino, Roswell, and in parts of Socorro County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Stallion Runway White Sands Missile Range station measured a peak wind gust of 65 mph. This likely originated from thunderstorm outflow, but no thunderstorms were in the immediate vicinity at the time of the report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increased moisture behind a backdoor front across eastern New Mexico led to the development of scattered storms across the east slopes of the central mountain chain growing upscale as they moved east. Some of the storms briefly became severe, producing strong wind gusts at Tucumcari, Roswell, and near Bitter Lake Wildlife Refuge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cannon Air Force Base.
