Severe storms fired up along a backdoor front moving south and west across eastern NM. One of these storms produced golf ball sized hail near Ramon along U.S. Highway 285.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five vehicles were disabled on U.S. Highway 285 and one occupant sustained cuts to her arms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher low-level moisture on July 13th and a backdoor cold front replenishing this moisture for July 14th and 15th combined with robust upper level wind shear produced three days of severe weather across eastern NM. Severe weather impacts were limited to northeastern NM on the 13th and 14th, becoming more widespread across northeastern and east-central NM on the 15th. There were a few thunderstorm wind gusts measured in the vicinity of the White Sands Missile Range on the 13th and 14th, likely from dry microbursts as the exception for the 13th and 14th. More substantial reports of damage and large hail occurred on the 15th ranging from broken skylights, flooding, accumulated hail, and a tornado. Thankfully, there were no reports of injuries or casualties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half-dollar size hail reported alongside larger but shorter duration 2.75 inch hail. Hail persisted until approximately 5:10 pm MDT before quitting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed over portions of eastern NM. One strong storm dropped nickel sized hail in Colfax County in the afternoon. Later in the evening, two severe storms produced strong wind gusts at the Tucumcari and Roswell airport observing sites.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail accumulation of 1 inch in the area resulted in two accidents on I-25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sufficient lift ahead of an upper low over California and Arizona and Gulf moisture ahead of the dryline across eastern New Mexico allowed widespread showers and storms to initiate around midday. Several supercells produced large and severe hail along with two tornadoes in the central and south-central highlands of Guadalupe and Lincoln County. Later that evening, training supercells grew upscale into a convective complex with flash flooding across east central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser reported a foot of fast moving water crossing NM Highway 252 at this location. No damages were reported.

Several consecutive days of significant severe weather impacted eastern New Mexico from May 24-26, 2023. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the dryline and east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on May 23rd. Storms tracked east and southeast across the eastern plains of New Mexico. Extensive severe weather was reported when these storms encountered greater moisture and lift closer to the Texas border. Hail up to 3.5 inches in diameter, thunderstorm wind gusts of 80 to 90 mph, and three tornados were reported in an area stretching from Union County to Roosevelt County. The largest hail stones and strongest wind gusts focused throughout Curry County during the evening hours. Hail and wind damage was reported in Tucumcari, Grady, and Clovis. Three separate tornadoes were reported in northern Curry County. Thunderstorms also dropped very heavy rainfall of 2 to 4 inches of rain across portions of Quay, De Baca, Curry, and Roosevelt Counties. Urban flooding occurred in Clovis, NM, causing some roads to be closed, and some cars to be stranded. Severe weather continued through the night and into May 25th with numerous reports of large hail, strong winds, and flash flooding. Quay County was particularly hard hit with baseball size hail and torrential rainfall amounts in excess of 5 inches. Significant hail damage was reported across Tucumcari with widespread flooding across the county. Severe wind gusts of 70 to 80 mph were common with these storms with damage reported. Strong to severe storms continued well into the night once again on the 25th with more heavy rainfall around the Caprock. The focus for severe weather shifted farther west on May 26th with large hail and heavy rainfall reported around Torrance County. A tornado and baseball size hail was also reported with a supercell near Encino. This activity spread eastward through the day with large hail, damaging wind gusts, and more torrential rainfall around the Caprock well into the night of May 26th. Three-day rainfall amounts exceeded 6 inches in many parts of Curry and Quay counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved across eastern NM as the wet pattern continued during the first half of June. Severe drought conditions improved across a wide portion of the eastern plains of NM along the Texas border thanks to regular rounds of thunderstorms during this period of June. There remained a section of severe drought isolated to the southern and southeastern tiers of Chaves County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Regular rounds of thunderstorms through the month of June improved the last remaining area of severe drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoon moisture surged into NM allowing for storms producing heavy rainfall and severe winds over portions of northeastern NM. Severe winds near Clayton, NM caused some damage on August 12th. Two more storms produced severe winds that were observed by automated weather stations at Santa Rosa and Tucumcari on August 13th. Heavy rainfall also produced flash flooding within the Hermit's Peak Calf Canyon burn scar during the afternoon. There were reports of debris and rocks washed across two state highways near Holman and Mora, and sandbags were deployed to protect homes in and around Rociada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe winds destroyed a wall and blew part of the roof off of an old horse barn. It also blew two fiberglass windows out of a metal barn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor front entered northeastern NM during the early morning hours increasing surface moisture across the region. This added moisture along with daytime heating allowed isolated supercells to develop across southeastern Colfax and eastern Mora counties, moving southeast towards the Texas state line. Storms produced up to golf ball sized hail in Springer and near Roy, and wind gusts of up to 74 mph at Tucumcari.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail fell with a rapidly developing supercell between Wagon Mound and Roy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoon moisture surged into NM allowing for storms producing heavy rainfall and severe winds over portions of northeastern NM. Severe winds near Clayton, NM caused some damage on August 12th. Two more storms produced severe winds that were observed by automated weather stations at Santa Rosa and Tucumcari on August 13th. Heavy rainfall also produced flash flooding within the Hermit's Peak Calf Canyon burn scar during the afternoon. There were reports of debris and rocks washed across two state highways near Holman and Mora, and sandbags were deployed to protect homes in and around Rociada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tucumcari Airport measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph.

Hail up to 3.5 inches in diameter, thunderstorm wind gusts of 80 to 90 mph, and three tornados were reported in an area stretching from Union County to Roosevelt County. The largest hail stones and strongest wind gusts focused throughout Curry County during the evening hours. Hail and wind damage was reported in Tucumcari, Grady, and Clovis. Three separate tornadoes were reported in northern Curry County. Thunderstorms also dropped very heavy rainfall of 2 to 4 inches of rain across portions of Quay, De Baca, Curry, and Roosevelt Counties. Urban flooding occurred in Clovis, NM, causing some roads to be closed, and some cars to be stranded. Severe weather continued through the night and into May 25th with numerous reports of large hail, strong winds, and flash flooding. Quay County was particularly hard hit with baseball size hail and torrential rainfall amounts in excess of 5 inches. Significant hail damage was reported across Tucumcari with widespread flooding across the county. Severe wind gusts of 70 to 80 mph were common with these storms with damage reported. Strong to severe storms continued well into the night once again on the 25th with more heavy rainfall around the Caprock. The focus for severe weather shifted farther west on May 26th with large hail and heavy rainfall reported around Torrance County. A tornado and baseball size hail was also reported with a supercell near Encino. This activity spread eastward through the day with large hail, damaging wind gusts, and more torrential rainfall around the Caprock well into the night of May 26th. Three-day rainfall amounts exceeded 6 inches in many parts of Curry and Quay counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a public report of 1 inch tree limbs down.

Sufficient lift ahead of an upper low over California and Arizona and Gulf moisture ahead of the dryline across eastern New Mexico allowed widespread showers and storms to initiate around midday. Several supercells produced large and severe hail along with two tornadoes in the central and south-central highlands of Guadalupe and Lincoln County. EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a social media report of quarter size hail at the Roswell Airport.

CZ_NAME: MIDDLE RIO GRANDE VALLEY/ALBUQUERQUE METRO AREA

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Convectively induced outflow from eastern NM produced an east canyon wind gust topping out at 58 mph at the Albuquerque Sunport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms across the Gila mountain range drifted north over western New Mexico through outflow boundaries. This allowed a decaying thunderstorm over Grants and a developing thunderstorm over Farmington to produce dry microbursts at the respective airport observing sites.

Storms across the Gila mountain range drifted north over western New Mexico through outflow boundaries. This allowed a decaying thunderstorm over Grants and a developing thunderstorm over Farmington to produce dry microbursts at the respective airport observing sites.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A decaying shower produced a dry microburst wind gust at the Grants airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms moving across the Four Corners area produced dry microbursts and significant damage at several locations across the Northwest Plateau in northwest NM. The Farmington observing station reported a wind gust of 50 knots or 58 miles per hour on the leading edge of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a downed utility pole from a likely strong gust front and microburst ahead of a line of storms moving east from Arizona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor front entered northeastern NM during the early morning hours increasing surface moisture across the region. This added moisture along with daytime heating allowed isolated supercells to develop across southeastern Colfax and eastern Mora counties, moving southeast towards the Texas state line. Storms produced up to golf ball sized hail in Springer and near Roy, and wind gusts of up to 74 mph at Tucumcari.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell moving southeast produced quarter to golf ball sized hail in Springer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon thunderstorms produced heavy rain and hail in the form of 1 to 2 plus inches of rainfall in the Albuquerque metro area. This resulted in numerous reports of flash flooding in the northeastern area of Albuquerque. Strong wind gusts were also observed at sites across the White Sands Missile Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms produced heavy rain over the highlighted area that resulted in several reports of flash flooding impacts. Flash flooding forced debris over the roadway at the intersection Montgomery and Carlisle with rocks greater than 5 inches in diameter, and street flooding also observed at Moon and Menaul. High water rises were observed on area arroyos resulting in swift water rescue being deployed with two people able to self-rescue. I-40 westbound also saw two lanes close near San Pedro for emergency repairs. There was also 6 inches of standing water on the bridge over the Hahn Arroyo, with water also reaching the base of a pedestrian bridge over I-40 near San Mateo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were several significant heavy rain events that occurred over parts of eastern NM during the month of May. These events, while causing flooding impacts, also improved drought conditions across a wide swath of eastern NM. This resulted in the complete removal of extreme and exceptional drought conditions with the overall coverage of severe drought conditions also lowering some.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved with the removal of extreme and exceptional drought conditions across the county. There remained a portion of severe drought in the southeastern and western sections of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few storms developed along a dryline over eastern NM, in tandem with an upper level disturbance crossing the state. This allowed for a few strong thunderstorm cells to develop through the northeastern plains of NM. A few produced dime to quarter size hail over parts of northeastern NM. Only very minor damage was reported to vegetation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail covering the ground was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Harold moved through southern Texas and into the Borderland on August 23.  While the storm weakened significantly, some convective thunderstorm bands developed over Otero county on the afternoon of the 23rd and dropped over 3 inches of rain in La Luz. The heavy rainfall was also blamed for a landslide that occurred along Highway 82 near High Rolls during the early morning hours of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in La Luz recorded 3.33 inches of rain on the afternoon of the 23rd.  Additional heavy rain reports included 2.43 inches in Cloudcroft, 2.35 inches about a mile northwest of High Rolls and 2.14 inches in High Rolls. Several other locations in and around the Sacramento Mountains reported an inch and a half to 2 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Harold moved through southern Texas and into the Borderland on August 23.  While the storm weakened significantly, some convective thunderstorm bands developed over Otero county on the afternoon of the 23rd and dropped over 3 inches of rain in La Luz. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rock slide reported on Highway 82 just west of Mountain Park near the Tunnel Vista observation site due to the heavy rain that fell the previous afternoon/evening. The road remained closed for approximately 2 days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Harold lingered across southwest New Mexico with a weak surface low near the Continental Divide.  Isolated thunderstorms develop near the Arizona border and moved north across the Lordsburg Playa producing a wind gust to 68 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NMDOT Mesonet station near mile post 7 on Interstate 10 recorded a peak gust of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dry Monsoon Season across the southwest lead to drought conditions worsening across the region.  The month of August started with most areas in Abnormally Dry(D0) drought or Moderate(D1) drought, but by the end of the month, most areas were at least in Severe(D2) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone started out in Abnormally Dry(D0) drought but on August 22, the western half of the zone was placed in Severe(D2) drought and the east in Moderate(D1) drought. The White Signal Cooperative Observer only recorded 0.25 inches of precipitation which was 2.49 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved across eastern NM as the wet pattern continued during the first half of June. Severe drought conditions improved across a wide portion of the eastern plains of NM along the Texas border thanks to regular rounds of thunderstorms during this period of June. There remained a section of severe drought isolated to the southern and southeastern tiers of Chaves County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Regular rounds of thunderstorms through the month of June improved the last remaining area of severe drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating and moisture over eastern NM allowed storms to ignite over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains spreading southeast over the northeast and east central plains by the late afternoon to early evening hours. A few storms reached severe limits near Raton, Tucumcari, and Clovis. Storms moved east into Texas after 9 PM local time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported near Forrest, NM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper level low from May 31st slowly moved northeast towards Colorado with another round of severe storms across eastern New Mexico. Training supercells in central and southern Guadalupe County resulted in several reports of 1 to 2 inch hail as well as heavy rain. There were no reports of flash flooding however.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail fell 4 miles south of Milagro in western Guadalupe County in association with a supercell. Hail accumulations of up to 3 inches occurred at that location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level Pacific moisture wrapping around a strong area of high pressure over northern Mexico resulted in some virga showers and dry thunderstorms over central and eastern New Mexico. One of these dry thunderstorms produced a dry microburst at Cannon AFB.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry microburst at the automated weather station at Cannon Air Force Base KCVS.

This added moisture helped aid in the development of two rounds of showers and severe thunderstorms across the region lasting into the early morning hours of June 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter observed mostly penny-sized hailstones, but a few quarter-sized were observed.

CZ_NAME: MIDDLE RIO GRANDE VALLEY/ALBUQUERQUE METRO AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong backdoor cold front surged through the gaps of the central mountain chain during the very early morning of May 13, 2023. High winds produced repeated gusts to 60 mph at the Albuquerque Sunport. A tree was blown down in an Albuquerque neighborhood. Meanwhile, an unrelated brief landspout tornado was reported near Gallup by the acting fire chief of McKinley County. The landspout caused no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Albuquerque Sunport ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 61 mph. These winds downed a tree in the Northeast Heights area of the Albuquerque metro area.

Abundant monsoon moisture surged into NM allowing for storms producing heavy rainfall and severe winds over portions of northeastern NM. Severe winds near Clayton, NM caused some damage on August 12th. Two more storms produced severe winds that were observed by automated weather stations at Santa Rosa and Tucumcari on August 13th. Heavy rainfall also produced flash flooding within the Hermit's Peak Calf Canyon burn scar during the afternoon. There were reports of debris and rocks washed across two state highways near Holman and Mora, and sandbags were deployed to protect homes in and around Rociada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Santa Rosa Airport measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a disturbance over CO along with healthy low-level moisture resulted in severe storms developing across the eastern plains of New Mexico. These cells produced quarter sized hail at times. Cells congealed into squall lines as they approached the Texas border resulting in severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Melrose AWOS station measured a 59 mph thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions overall deteriorated across the state due to an incredibly dry and hot start to the monsoon season. Severe drought expanded across northwest New Mexico in parts of the Northwest Highlands and Far Northwest Highlands. Severe drought also expanded westward across portions of southeastern New Mexico, including the Chaves County Plains and Southwest Chaves County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very dry start to the monsoon season along with record heat in July resulted in the introduction of severe drought across a majority of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher low-level moisture on July 13th and a backdoor cold front replenishing this moisture for July 14th and 15th combined with robust upper level wind shear produced three days of severe weather across eastern NM. Severe weather impacts were limited to northeastern NM on the 13th and 14th, becoming more widespread across northeastern and east-central NM on the 15th. There were a few thunderstorm wind gusts measured in the vicinity of the White Sands Missile Range on the 13th and 14th, likely from dry microbursts as the exception for the 13th and 14th. More substantial reports of damage and large hail occurred on the 15th ranging from broken skylights, flooding, accumulated hail, and a tornado. Thankfully, there were no reports of injuries or casualties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long lasting hail with strong winds and numerous tree branches downed. A report on twitter also indicated large hail broke a plate glass window in a shop in Springer. Power went out and did not come back on until 3pm local time on July 14th. Damage is an estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dry Monsoon Season across the southwest lead to drought conditions worsening across the region.  The month of August started with most areas in Abnormally Dry(D0) drought or Moderate(D1) drought, but by the end of the month, most areas were at least in Severe(D2) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month started with Abnormally Dry(D0) drought north and Moderate(D1) drought south.  On August 22, the zone was placed in a mix of Severe(D2) and Moderate(D1) drought. The Truth or Consequences ASOS reported 0.73 inches of precipitation which was 1.03 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several consecutive days of significant severe weather impacted eastern New Mexico from May 24-26, 2023. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the dryline and east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on May 23rd. Storms tracked east and southeast across the eastern plains of New Mexico. Extensive severe weather was reported when these storms encountered greater moisture and lift closer to the Texas border. Hail up to 3.5 inches in diameter, thunderstorm wind gusts of 80 to 90 mph, and three tornados were reported in an area stretching from Union County to Roosevelt County. The largest hail stones and strongest wind gusts focused throughout Curry County during the evening hours. Hail and wind damage was reported in Tucumcari, Grady, and Clovis. Three separate tornadoes were reported in northern Curry County. Thunderstorms also dropped very heavy rainfall of 2 to 4 inches of rain across portions of Quay, De Baca, Curry, and Roosevelt Counties. Urban flooding occurred in Clovis, NM, causing some roads to be closed, and some cars to be stranded. Severe weather continued through the night and into May 25th with numerous reports of large hail, strong winds, and flash flooding. Quay County was particularly hard hit with baseball size hail and torrential rainfall amounts in excess of 5 inches. Significant hail damage was reported across Tucumcari with widespread flooding across the county. Severe wind gusts of 70 to 80 mph were common with these storms with damage reported. Strong to severe storms continued well into the night once again on the 25th with more heavy rainfall around the Caprock. The focus for severe weather shifted farther west on May 26th with large hail and heavy rainfall reported around Torrance County. A tornado and baseball size hail was also reported with a supercell near Encino. This activity spread eastward through the day with large hail, damaging wind gusts, and more torrential rainfall around the Caprock well into the night of May 26th. Three-day rainfall amounts exceeded 6 inches in many parts of Curry and Quay counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NM Department of Transportation reported State Highway 108 was closed to flooding north of Pleasant Hill.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire ignited in the town of Sapello, NM just outside of the recent Hermit's Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar. The fire was discovered around 4 pm MDT along Highway 94 just west of Sapello. The fire resulted in road closures, mandatory evacuations for residents in Canoncito through May 12, 2023, and structures burned. A red flag warning was in effect for this area at the time of ignition for critical fire weather conditions. The fire was ninety percent contained by May 15, 2023 as calmer winds and higher moisture settled in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Las Tusas Fire was discovered May 10, 2023 around 4 pm MDT just west of Sapello. The fire caused mandatory evacuations and burned several structures. Total acreage burned was 978 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sufficient lift ahead of an upper low over California and Arizona and Gulf moisture ahead of the dryline across eastern New Mexico allowed widespread showers and storms to initiate around midday. Several supercells produced large and severe hail along with two tornadoes in the central and south-central highlands of Guadalupe and Lincoln County. Later that evening, training supercells grew upscale into a convective complex with flash flooding across east central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were two reports of dime and quarter size hail a few miles west of Fort Sumner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms moving across the Four Corners area produced dry microbursts and significant damage at several locations across the Northwest Plateau in northwest NM. The Farmington observing station reported a wind gust of 50 knots or 58 miles per hour on the leading edge of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A greenhouse was damaged from a strong gust front and likely microburst ahead of a line of storms moving east from Arizona.

The Farmington observing station reported a wind gust of 50 knots or 58 miles per hour on the leading edge of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two utility poles were damaged at the Navajo Dam from strong winds. This was likely caused by a dry microburst ahead of storms moving in from the west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance passing over CO combined with sufficient low-level moisture allowed for scattered storms to develop over northeastern NM during the afternoon of July 19th. These scattered storms stayed over very rural areas through the mid-to-late afternoon period likely producing strong winds and large hail. There was only one report of an estimated severe wind gust just east of Gladstone, NM however.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer estimated a 70 mph wind gust along with some pea size hail. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered diurnal thunderstorms across southeast New Mexico resulted in localized strong winds.
Scattered thunderstorms ahead of the remnants of tropical storm Harold resulted in localized severe winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed by a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A landspout was reported with diurnal afternoon convection across portions of southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: LANDSPOUT. Location: 32.8166N, 104.4066W. Delayed report of an apparent landspout tornado on the west side of Artesia. Multiple photos and videos were shared of this landspout. Location is an estimation based on photo evidence to be 1 SSW of Artesia. No damage was reported and the duration is unknown.

The focus for severe weather shifted farther west on May 26th with large hail and heavy rainfall reported around Torrance County. A tornado and baseball size hail was also reported with a supercell near Encino. This activity spread eastward through the day with large hail, damaging wind gusts, and more torrential rainfall around the Caprock well into the night of May 26th. Three-day rainfall amounts exceeded 6 inches in many parts of Curry and Quay counties.

Several consecutive days of significant severe weather impacted eastern New Mexico from May 24-26, 2023. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the dryline and east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on May 23rd. Storms tracked east and southeast across the eastern plains of New Mexico. Extensive severe weather was reported when these storms encountered greater moisture and lift closer to the Texas border. Hail up to 3.5 inches in diameter, thunderstorm wind gusts of 80 to 90 mph, and three tornados were reported in an area stretching from Union County to Roosevelt County. Three separate tornadoes were reported in northern Curry County. Urban flooding occurred in Clovis, NM, causing some roads to be closed, and some cars to be stranded. Quay County was particularly hard hit with baseball size hail and torrential rainfall amounts in excess of 5 inches. Strong to severe storms continued well into the night once again on the 25th with more heavy rainfall around the Caprock. Drought conditions improved across eastern NM as the wet pattern continued during the first half of June. Severe drought conditions improved across a wide portion of the eastern plains of NM along the Texas border thanks to regular rounds of thunderstorms during this period of June. There remained a section of severe drought isolated to the southern and southeastern tiers of Chaves County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Regular rounds of thunderstorms through the month of June improved the last remaining area of severe drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher low-level moisture on July 13th and a backdoor cold front replenishing this moisture for July 14th and 15th combined with robust upper level wind shear produced three days of severe weather across eastern NM. Severe weather impacts were limited to northeastern NM on the 13th and 14th, becoming more widespread across northeastern and east-central NM on the 15th. There were a few thunderstorm wind gusts measured in the vicinity of the White Sands Missile Range on the 13th and 14th, likely from dry microbursts as the exception for the 13th and 14th. More substantial reports of damage and large hail occurred on the 15th ranging from broken skylights, flooding, accumulated hail, and a tornado. Thankfully, there were no reports of injuries or casualties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cone tornado was reported by storm chasers just southeast of the intersection of NM 39 and U.S. Highway 56. The tornado lasted three minutes and was rain-wrapped for a time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system accompanied by a strong portion of the jetstream brought high winds to portions of central and eastern NM. These southwest winds reached nearly the entire state with high wind gusts being reported from Santa Fe to Albuquerque and through the highlands from Las Vegas to Clines Corners and Vaughn. Areas of blowing dust resulted from these winds, slightly dropping visibilities. The high winds also aided in the rapid spread of a new wildfire near Sapello, NM during the afternoon. There were no reports of damage or injuries directly related to the high winds. Meanwhile, severe weather broke out across far eastern NM along the TX border during the afternoon and evening hours. Thunderstorm activity favored a dryline feature with individual storm cells producing mostly pea to nickel size hail and severe wind gusts. Several severe wind gusts were reported in portions of northeastern NM with one report of one inch size hail near Clovis. Nickel size hail was also reported in San Jon, NM. There were no reports of wind or hail damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A social media post showed a photo of a measured 1 inch hail stone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong backdoor cold front surged through the gaps of the central mountain chain during the very early morning of May 13, 2023. High winds produced repeated gusts to 60 mph at the Albuquerque Sunport. A tree was blown down in an Albuquerque neighborhood. Meanwhile, an unrelated brief landspout tornado was reported near Gallup by the acting fire chief of McKinley County. The landspout caused no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The acting fire chief of McKinley County sent a video of a brief landspout tornado near Gallup. It caused no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near-record to record level moisture over parts of the region allowed for scattered to numerous storms to develop over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on May 18, 2023. Combined with slow storm motions, locally heavy rainfall occurred over portions of the Hermit's Peak Calf Canyon burn scar. This led to flash flooding near Guadalupita, NM at the extreme northeastern edge of the burn scar. Drainages below Silva Canyon on the Hermits Peak-Calf Canyon burn scar flowed out of banks and flooded nearby driveways and properties near SR-434 south of Guadalupita, NM. There were no reports of damage or loss of life.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twitter report of flash flooding near Guadalupita within the Hermit's Peak Calf Canyon burn scar. Drainage below Silva Canyon on the burn scar flowed out of the banks and flooded nearby driveways and properties near SR-434 south of Guadalupita. Damages are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sufficient lift ahead of an upper low over California and Arizona and Gulf moisture ahead of the dryline across eastern New Mexico allowed widespread showers and storms to initiate around midday. Several supercells produced large and severe hail along with two tornadoes in the central and south-central highlands of Guadalupe and Lincoln County. Later that evening, training supercells grew upscale into a convective complex with flash flooding across east central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NM Department of Highways reported NM Highway 278 closed from flooding due to heavy rain.

Monsoon thunderstorms produced heavy rain and hail in the form of 1 to 2 plus inches of rainfall in the Albuquerque metro area. This resulted in numerous reports of flash flooding in the northeastern area of Albuquerque. Strong wind gusts were also observed at sites across the White Sands Missile Range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One severe storm in Colorado moved southeast into Union County producing up to golf ball sized hail in Des Moines and penny to half dollar sized hail in Clayton. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home window was knocked out by copious amounts of golf ball size hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundary induced wind gust.

Hail up to 3.5 inches in diameter, thunderstorm wind gusts of 80 to 90 mph, and three tornados were reported in an area stretching from Union County to Roosevelt County. The largest hail stones and strongest wind gusts focused throughout Curry County during the evening hours. Hail and wind damage was reported in Tucumcari, Grady, and Clovis. Three separate tornadoes were reported in northern Curry County. Thunderstorms also dropped very heavy rainfall of 2 to 4 inches of rain across portions of Quay, De Baca, Curry, and Roosevelt Counties. Urban flooding occurred in Clovis, NM, causing some roads to be closed, and some cars to be stranded. Severe weather continued through the night and into May 25th with numerous reports of large hail, strong winds, and flash flooding. Quay County was particularly hard hit with baseball size hail and torrential rainfall amounts in excess of 5 inches. Significant hail damage was reported across Tucumcari with widespread flooding across the county. Severe wind gusts of 70 to 80 mph were common with these storms with damage reported. Strong to severe storms continued well into the night once again on the 25th with more heavy rainfall around the Caprock. The focus for severe weather shifted farther west on May 26th with large hail and heavy rainfall reported around Torrance County. A tornado and baseball size hail was also reported with a supercell near Encino. This activity spread eastward through the day with large hail, damaging wind gusts, and more torrential rainfall around the Caprock well into the night of May 26th. Three-day rainfall amounts exceeded 6 inches in many parts of Curry and Quay counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a CoCoRaHS report of 1.5 inch hail that caused unknown minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions overall deteriorated across the state due to an incredibly dry and hot start to the monsoon season. Severe drought expanded across northwest New Mexico in parts of the Northwest Highlands and Far Northwest Highlands. Severe drought also expanded westward across portions of southeastern New Mexico, including the Chaves County Plains and Southwest Chaves County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A late onset to the monsoon along with a record warm and dry July allowed severe A very dry start to the monsoon season along with record heat in July resulted in the expansion of severe drought across the southern half of the zone, reaching northward to near Roswell.

Well above average snowpack and warm temperatures led to rapid snowmelt and river rises on many northern NM river basins. Overbank flooding was reported at and downstream of the junction between the Rio Ojo Caliente and Rio Chama between May 5th and May 10th. Ten properties had water up to homes and structures. Yards, wells, and septic systems were flooded out. There were no reports of casualties or fatalities or water rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid snow melt led to high river rises and overbank flooding at and downstream of the junction between the Rio Ojo Caliente and Rio Chama. Ten homes had water come right up to the house structure. This flooded yards, wells and septic tanks. Damages are an estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an upper level system and an influx of low-level moisture allowed scattered strong storms to develop through southeastern and east-central NM during the afternoon of May 3, 2023. One storm was reported to have produced penny size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter drove through penny sized hail with the first storm to hit the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low off the coast of California helped funnel in subtropical moisture into the state resulting in the development of widespread showers and storms across central and eastern New Mexico June 5th and 6th. Reports of severe weather and flash flooding stretched from the Rio Grande Valley across portions of eastern NM on June 5th. These reports ranged from a landspout tornado near the Petroglyph National Monument and Nara Visa, NM to flash flooding. Another round of severe weather began in the afternoon of June 6th producing more widespread reports over the same area of the state. Reports ranged from burn scar flash flooding within the Hermits Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar and Santa Rosa, to large hail over parts of San Miguel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding reported across Santa Rosa via NM State EOC and County EM. Extent of impacts and potential damages are unknown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Landspout (non-mesocyclonic) tornado touched down briefly southeast of Nara Visa, NM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoon moisture surged into NM allowing for storms producing heavy rainfall and severe winds over portions of northeastern NM. Severe winds near Clayton, NM caused some damage on August 12th. Two more storms produced severe winds that were observed by automated weather stations at Santa Rosa and Tucumcari on August 13th. Heavy rainfall also produced flash flooding within the Hermit's Peak Calf Canyon burn scar during the afternoon. There were reports of debris and rocks washed across two state highways near Holman and Mora, and sandbags were deployed to protect homes in and around Rociada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Debris flow across State Highway 518 in Holman required removal of rocks and mud across the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Evening rain and storms caused flash flooding over the southern part of the Hermits Peak Calf Canyon burn scar at San Ignacio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Flood survey found evidence of multiple mud/debris flow overs County Road A3A. There was also evidence of water over a bridge and culvert. Debris left behind by the waters was stuck against guard rails.

A few severe storms developed over Union County during the late afternoon to early evening hours coinciding with peak heating. The storms moved southeast into the Texas and Oklahoma panhandles following the northwest upper-level flow. There were multiple hail reports ranging in size from pennies to half-dollars. Hail accumulation and heavy rain in Clayton caused some minor flooding impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twitter report of pea to quarter size hail just east of Clayton, NM from first supercell. Also, a report from the local fire and rescue of penny to nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor front brought moist easterly upslope flow into northeastern NM on June 11th and June 12th. Discrete supercells fired up along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains during the early afternoon hours of June 11th dropping large to destructive hail as they translated east towards the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles. More storms developed off the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains around sunset dropping more large to destructive hail across the region during the overnight hours into Monday morning. Storms eventually trained over the same areas resulting in major flash flooding over Colfax, Union, Mora and Harding Counties. More discrete supercells fired up midday June 12th tracking east before exiting into the Texas Panhandle around sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clayton PD reported a 911 call from a person in Gladstone whose car window was fractured by large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance tracked along the NM and CO border producing a few severe storms in northern Colfax and Union Counties. The storms produced some quarter to ping pong ball sized hail and some damaging wind gusts of up to 70 mph. Storms quickly moved east into Texas and Oklahoma during the evening hours. The system also produced strong westerly winds across central NM thanks to deep boundary layer mixing with one high wind gust at Clines Corners during the mid-afternoon hours.

A residence also sustained roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher low-level moisture on July 13th and a backdoor cold front replenishing this moisture for July 14th and 15th combined with robust upper level wind shear produced three days of severe weather across eastern NM. Severe weather impacts were limited to northeastern NM on the 13th and 14th, becoming more widespread across northeastern and east-central NM on the 15th. There were a few thunderstorm wind gusts measured in the vicinity of the White Sands Missile Range on the 13th and 14th, likely from dry microbursts as the exception for the 13th and 14th. More substantial reports of damage and large hail occurred on the 15th ranging from broken skylights, flooding, accumulated hail, and a tornado. Thankfully, there were no reports of injuries or casualties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser reported flooding on secondary roads near I-40 west of Newkirk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of monsoon season for New Mexico saw continued near to below normal precipitation over much of the area. As a result, extreme drought expanded across sections of northwestern NM and into portions of the middle Rio Grande Valley that include the Albuquerque metro area. Exceptional drought developed over parts of Chaves County near Roswell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of a dry monsoon season and above normal temperatures allowed for the introduction of extreme drought conditions in the southwestern half of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and dry conditions continued during the month of August across northern and central New Mexico. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of a dry monsoon season and above normal temperatures allowed for the expansion of severe drought conditions into southern and northeastern portions of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoon moisture surged into NM allowing for storms producing heavy rainfall and severe winds over portions of northeastern NM. Severe winds near Clayton, NM caused some damage on August 12th. Two more storms produced severe winds that were observed by automated weather stations at Santa Rosa and Tucumcari on August 13th. Heavy rainfall also produced flash flooding within the Hermit's Peak Calf Canyon burn scar during the afternoon. There were reports of debris and rocks washed across two state highways near Holman and Mora, and sandbags were deployed to protect homes in and around Rociada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southwestern Electric Cooperative reported severe winds having knocked down six power poles along State Highway 56 between mile markers 13 and 14 southwest of Clayton. Power was lost for a few hours. Damage is an estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon storms over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains slowly moved south across the Hermits Peak Calf Canyon Burn Scar resulting in some flash flooding at Lower Rociada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road leading up to residence was covered in flowing water near State Roads 105 and 276 in lower Rociada.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front and shortwave trough across eastern New Mexico helped storms initiate over the central mountain chain during the early afternoon hours. These storms produced rapid flash flooding over the McBride burn scar in Ruidoso. As storms moved east and south off the high terrain, they eventually congealed into a line with strong wind gusts reported at Encino, Roswell, and in parts of Socorro County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding at Gavilan Canyon Road and Warrior Drive. County roads in the area were closed shortly after. Additionally, a USGS image in the area depicted significant debris flow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor front brought moist easterly upslope flow into northeastern NM on June 11th and June 12th. Discrete supercells fired up along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains during the early afternoon hours of June 11th dropping large to destructive hail as they translated east towards the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles. More storms developed off the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains around sunset dropping more large to destructive hail across the region during the overnight hours into Monday morning. Storms eventually trained over the same areas resulting in major flash flooding over Colfax, Union, Mora and Harding Counties. More discrete supercells fired up midday June 12th tracking east before exiting into the Texas Panhandle around sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball size hail fell as a supercell moved east across northern Union County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance tracked along the NM and CO border producing a few severe storms in northern Colfax and Union Counties. The storms produced some quarter to ping pong ball sized hail and some damaging wind gusts of up to 70 mph. Storms quickly moved east into Texas and Oklahoma during the evening hours. The system also produced strong westerly winds across central NM thanks to deep boundary layer mixing with one high wind gust at Clines Corners during the mid-afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell on the west side of Des Moines as a supercell moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms moving across the Four Corners area produced dry microbursts and significant damage at several locations across the Northwest Plateau in northwest NM. The Farmington observing station reported a wind gust of 50 knots or 58 miles per hour on the leading edge of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust reported at KFMN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper level low from May 31st slowly moved northeast towards Colorado with another round of severe storms across eastern New Mexico. Training supercells in central and southern Guadalupe County resulted in several reports of 1 to 2 inch hail as well as heavy rain. There were no reports of flash flooding however.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 7 miles west-southwest of Kewa Pueblo reported nickel size hail in association with a supercell in eastern Sandoval County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper level low from May 31st slowly moved northeast towards Colorado with another round of severe storms across eastern New Mexico. Training supercells in central and southern Guadalupe County resulted in several reports of 1 to 2 inch hail as well as heavy rain. There were no reports of flash flooding however.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail accumulated and covered NM State Highway 219, three miles north-northeast of Pastura in central Guadalupe County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several consecutive days of significant severe weather impacted eastern New Mexico from May 24-26, 2023. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the dryline and east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on May 23rd. Storms tracked east and southeast across the eastern plains of New Mexico. Extensive severe weather was reported when these storms encountered greater moisture and lift closer to the Texas border. Hail up to 3.5 inches in diameter, thunderstorm wind gusts of 80 to 90 mph, and three tornados were reported in an area stretching from Union County to Roosevelt County. The largest hail stones and strongest wind gusts focused throughout Curry County during the evening hours. Hail and wind damage was reported in Tucumcari, Grady, and Clovis. Three separate tornadoes were reported in northern Curry County. Thunderstorms also dropped very heavy rainfall of 2 to 4 inches of rain across portions of Quay, De Baca, Curry, and Roosevelt Counties. Urban flooding occurred in Clovis, NM, causing some roads to be closed, and some cars to be stranded. Severe weather continued through the night and into May 25th with numerous reports of large hail, strong winds, and flash flooding. Quay County was particularly hard hit with baseball size hail and torrential rainfall amounts in excess of 5 inches. Significant hail damage was reported across Tucumcari with widespread flooding across the county. Severe wind gusts of 70 to 80 mph were common with these storms with damage reported. Strong to severe storms continued well into the night once again on the 25th with more heavy rainfall around the Caprock. The focus for severe weather shifted farther west on May 26th with large hail and heavy rainfall reported around Torrance County. A tornado and baseball size hail was also reported with a supercell near Encino. This activity spread eastward through the day with large hail, damaging wind gusts, and more torrential rainfall around the Caprock well into the night of May 26th. Three-day rainfall amounts exceeded 6 inches in many parts of Curry and Quay counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were three reports of severe hail ranging in size from 1.5 to 1.75 inches in Portales.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms moving across the Four Corners area produced dry microbursts and significant damage at several locations across the Northwest Plateau in northwest NM. The Farmington observing station reported a wind gust of 50 knots or 58 miles per hour on the leading edge of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in Kiwanis Park from a likely microburst. Individual heard a huge crack when trees fell. The microburst also came with a wall of sand and a possible dust devil.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed along the Continental Divide and then moved east into the Rio Grande Valley. A strong and slow moving storm caused considerable flash flooding across Albuquerque.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video shared on social media by the local media showed that heavy rain caused a waterfall over a hillside below the Petroglyph National Monument. A cul-de-sac near Taylor Ranch was also inundated with hail and heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor front brought moist easterly upslope flow into northeastern NM on June 11th and June 12th. Discrete supercells fired up along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains during the early afternoon hours of June 11th dropping large to destructive hail as they translated east towards the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles. More storms developed off the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains around sunset dropping more large to destructive hail across the region during the overnight hours into Monday morning. Storms eventually trained over the same areas resulting in major flash flooding over Colfax, Union, Mora and Harding Counties. More discrete supercells fired up midday June 12th tracking east before exiting into the Texas Panhandle around sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from mPING of hen egg size hail as supercell moves east through northern Quay County.

Northwest flow rounding the northeastern periphery of an upper high over AZ combined with abundant low-level moisture over the high plains of eastern NM to produce severe weather along and east of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on July 21st and 22nd. Large hail and severe winds were observed from these scattered storm cells. Heavy rainfall also produced flash flooding on the Hermit's Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar on July 21st. Fast flowing flood waters with burn scar debris was observed at several locations causing damage to properties and forcing two road closures north and south of Mora, NM. Flash flooding was also reported near the Ute Park burn scar along the Ponil Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding within the Hermit's Peak Calf Canyon burn scar along state highway 434 at mile markers 4 and 5, closing the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of monsoon season for New Mexico saw continued near to below normal precipitation over much of the area. As a result, extreme drought expanded across sections of northwestern NM and into portions of the middle Rio Grande Valley that include the Albuquerque metro area. Exceptional drought developed over parts of Chaves County near Roswell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of a dry monsoon season and above normal temperatures allowed for the expansion of severe drought conditions into the southwestern portion of the zone.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A resident just west of Capulin shared a photo of a funnel cloud from a strong thunderstorm.

Training supercells in central and southern Guadalupe County resulted in several reports of 1 to 2 inch hail as well as heavy rain. There were no reports of flash flooding however.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell 5 miles south-southeast of Puerto De Luna in central Guadalupe County initially produced ping pong ball size hail, growing to 2 inch size hail 13 minutes later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance passed over northern NM helping to invigorate another round of afternoon thunderstorms along and east of the central mountain chain of NM. This isolated to scattered activity covered an area stretching from the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains across much of eastern NM. Heavy rains over and near the Hermit's Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar caused flash flooding in the Coyote Creek State Park. Strong to severe storms also produced large hail ranging from Mora County to Roosevelt County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local emergency manager relayed reports of flash flooding over State Highway 434 at several locations between mile markers 7 and 10. There was also flooding reported over nearby county roads, including roads A034, A020, and B022 near Guadalupita and Coyote Creek State Park. No damage was reported.

This isolated to scattered activity covered an area stretching from the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains across much of eastern NM. Heavy rains over and near the Hermit's Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar caused flash flooding in the Coyote Creek State Park. An upper-level low off the coast of California helped funnel in subtropical moisture into the state resulting in the development of widespread showers and storms across central and eastern New Mexico June 5th and 6th. Reports of severe weather and flash flooding stretched from the Rio Grande Valley across portions of eastern NM on June 5th. These reports ranged from a landspout tornado near the Petroglyph National Monument and Nara Visa, NM to flash flooding. Another round of severe weather began in the afternoon of June 6th producing more widespread reports over the same area of the state. Reports ranged from burn scar flash flooding within the Hermits Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar and Santa Rosa, to large hail over parts of San Miguel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain and quarter sized hail falling at the time of report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few storms developed along a dryline over eastern NM, in tandem with an upper level disturbance crossing the state. This allowed for a few strong thunderstorm cells to develop through the northeastern plains of NM. A few produced dime to quarter size hail over parts of northeastern NM. Only very minor damage was reported to vegetation.

An upper level disturbance passing over CO combined with sufficient low-level moisture allowed for scattered storms to develop over northeastern NM during the afternoon of July 19th. These scattered storms stayed over very rural areas through the mid-to-late afternoon period likely producing strong winds and large hail. There was only one report of an estimated severe wind gust just east of Gladstone, NM however.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fort Sumner Airport measured a peak thunderstorm outflow wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front and shortwave trough across eastern New Mexico helped storms initiate over the central mountain chain during the early afternoon hours. These storms produced rapid flash flooding over the McBride burn scar in Ruidoso. As storms moved east and south off the high terrain, they eventually congealed into a line with strong wind gusts reported at Encino, Roswell, and in parts of Socorro County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail fell in Maxwell as a cell moved northeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed along a sharpening dryline across eastern NM. Storms strengthened as they tracked eastward toward the Texas border, producing large hail and even a landspout tornado. Hail reports ranged in size from pennies to golf balls and were focused across Quay and Curry counties. The landspout was reported just south of Texico and caused no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty National Weather Service employee reported a landspout that began near County Road 6 and Curry Road B and moved south to near County Road 5. There was no damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dry Monsoon Season across the southwest lead to drought conditions worsening across the region.  The month of August started with most areas in Abnormally Dry(D0) drought or Moderate(D1) drought, but by the end of the month, most areas were at least in Severe(D2) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone went from Abnormally Dry(D0) drought to Severe(D2) drought on August 22. The Cooperative Observer in Cliff reported 1.43 inches of precipitation which was 1.02 inches below normal.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SACRAMENTO MOUNTAINS BELOW 7500 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry Monsoon Season brought worsening drought conditions into Extreme(D3) drought for extreme eastern Otero County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month started with Severe(D2) drought south and Moderate(D1) drought north but on August 1st was placed in Extreme(D3) drought. The Cooperative Observer just outside Otero county in Elk reported 3.66 inches of precipitation which was 0.92 inches above normal after 1.56 inches of rain fell August 23-25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry Monsoon Season brought worsening drought conditions into Extreme(D3) drought for extreme eastern Otero County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month started with Severe(D2) drought in the east and Moderate(D1) drought west.  On August 22, the eastern half was placed in Extreme(D3) drought with Severe(D2) drought west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and dry conditions continued during the month of August across northern and central New Mexico. The lack of monsoon showers and storms allowed drought conditions to continue to deteriorate areawide with most of the region under severe to extreme drought, outside of parts of west central New Mexico and most of northeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of a dry monsoon season and above normal temperatures allowed for the expansion of severe drought conditions into most of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall drought conditions did not change much across northern and central NM during the month of November. Near to above normal precipitation was observed through the west-central and central swath of NM and this allowed for a marginal one categorical improvement in drought conditions for some of this area. Meanwhile, below normal to zero precipitation was observed over the northern mountains along the CO border and northeastern plains. This resulted in a marginal one categorical degradation in drought conditions for a portion of this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dry and warm November resulted in the persistence of extreme drought conditions over a majority of the zone and exceptional drought in the southern part of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly high winds occurred across the Guadalupe and Delaware Mountains and adjacent plains as a shortwave trough moved across the southern Great Plains.
A brief breakdown of the persistent upper ridge due to a disturbance moving southeast through New Mexico brought isolated severe thunderstorms to Grant and Otero counties with hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Silver City AWOS recorded a peak gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief breakdown of the persistent upper ridge due to a disturbance moving southeast through New Mexico brought isolated severe thunderstorms to Grant and Otero counties with hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail reported just northeast of Mescalero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface low was located just south of the New Mexico Bootheel while a disturbance associated with an upper trough crossing the Great Lakes. A large temperature/dew point spread aided in strong wind gusts which crossed the Lordsburg Playa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NMDOT site on the Lordsburg Playa recorded a peak gust of 61 mph which created areas of blowing dust reducing visibilities to under a half mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough was located along the Rio Grande Valley with southeast surface winds bringing in modest moisture to the Sacramento Mountains. A weak disturbance moving through northwest flow aloft provided enough lift for a strong thunderstorm to drop hail up to the size of pennies near Cloudcroft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near Silver Lake reported hail up to the size of pennies.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: July was the hottest on record for Southern New Mexico as an upper ridge persisted around the Borderland throughout the month.  High temperatures at New Mexico State University topped 100 degrees 28 of the 31 days and the average temperature was 88.2 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature at the New Mexico State University Cooperative Observer station was 88.2 degrees which was 2.5 degrees warmer than the previous record set in 2016.  The high temperature topped 100 degrees 28 of 31 days during the month with only 0.30 inches of precipitation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature at the White Sands National Park Cooperative Observer station was 87.4 degrees which was 1.7 degrees warmer than the previous record set in 1951.  The high temperature topped 100 degrees nearly every day during the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature at the Deming Airport was 87.2 degrees which was 2.8 degrees warmer than the record set in 2016.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent backdoor cold front surged southward into northeastern NM on November 20th. Aided by strong winds aloft and mountain wave activity allowing these winds to crash to the surface south of Raton Pass, the Raton Airport measured a peak wind gust of 63 mph. These winds also combined with blowing snow to cause hazardous winter travel conditions from I-25 eastward toward Folsom along New Mexico Highway 72.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raton Airport measured a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall drought conditions did not change much across northern and central NM during the month of November. Near to above normal precipitation was observed through the west-central and central swath of NM and this allowed for a marginal one categorical improvement in drought conditions for some of this area. Meanwhile, below normal to zero precipitation was observed over the northern mountains along the CO border and northeastern plains. This resulted in a marginal one categorical degradation in drought conditions for a portion of this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dry and warm November resulted in the the introduction of severe drought to the entirety of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough across the southern Rocky Mountains helped develop afternoon strong to severe thunderstorms across the higher elevations and adjacent plains. Isolated severe wind gusts were reported.
Isolated diurnal afternoon thunderstorms resulted in localized strong to severe wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale weather system interacted with an unstable and moist airmass to produce severe storms over parts of eastern NM. Accumulating hail and minor flooding were observed in Las Vegas, along with a severe thunderstorm wind gust at Tucumcari.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy accumulations of dime to nickel size hail was observed on the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dry and warm October after a record dry monsoon season allowed drought conditions to persist across New Mexico. Generally, drought conditions remained steady. A patch of exceptional drought was introduced in the northwest highlands and northwest Jemez mountains, and severe drought conditions expanded eastward across east central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dry and warm October resulted in the persistence of severe drought conditions for most of the zone and introduction of extreme drought in the far southwestern section of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale weather system interacted with an unstable and moist airmass to produce severe storms over parts of eastern NM. Accumulating hail and minor flooding were observed in Las Vegas, along with a severe thunderstorm wind gust at Tucumcari.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The District Ranger for the U.S. Forest Service in Las Vegas reported half-dollar sized hail at their district office building. Hail damage to the roof was reported with a leak resulting from the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level storm system tracked along the CO/WY border with an associated strong jetmax moving over New Mexico. Some of this energy reached the surface in the form of high winds that impacted portions of central highlands, south-central mountains, Guadalupe and Quay Counties on October 12th. The first of these high winds hit the south-central mountains in the pre-dawn hours with parts of the eastern plains of NM seeing their strongest wind gusts in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clines Corners ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gust was recorded near the White Sands Missile Range Main Post.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued moist southerly flow across eastern NM ahead of a deep storm system resulted in the development of more scattered showers and storms across eastern NM. Some of these storms became severe, with one of these storms producing a tornado near Sapello in northwest San Miguel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage assessment conducted by San Miguel Emergency Management discovered damage to a roof of one house, an overturned chicken coop near the damaged house, and power poles downed between Escondido Lane and Wyatt Dr. intersecting County Road A1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers with embedded thunderstorms developed across central and southern New Mexico ahead of an upper-level low pressure system over northern Mexico. Outflow winds ahead of this activity produced a strong wind gust of 58 mph at the Stallion Runway mesonet on the White Sands Missile Range grounds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stallion Runway station measured a 58 mph thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall drought conditions did not change much across northern and central NM during the month of November. Near to above normal precipitation was observed through the west-central and central swath of NM and this allowed for a marginal one categorical improvement in drought conditions for some of this area. Meanwhile, below normal to zero precipitation was observed over the northern mountains along the CO border and northeastern plains. This resulted in a marginal one categorical degradation in drought conditions for a portion of this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A warm and dry November resulted in the introduction of extreme drought conditions in the majority of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms, each weaker but colder than the one before passed over New Mexico from November 30th to December 2nd. The first system was well defined and passed west to east mainly along I-40 producing several inches up to a foot of snow at elevations above seven thousand feet by the morning of November 30th. The most severe impacts were travel related on the I-40 and I-25 corridors through the East Mountain Communities and Santa Fe. Snow accumulations of 4 to 10 inches in these areas produced car accidents along I-40 near Rancho Verde and shutdown of I-25 from Santa Fe to Glorieta Pass. The second and third storm system were much weaker and only produced additional snowfall at higher elevations with no further impacts being reported on December 1st and 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cibola County Emergency Manager reported 13 inches of snow just west of Mt. Taylor. Heavy snow lead to impacts to travel along I-40 from Thoreau to Gallup as reported on social media. Four to seven inches of snow were also reported along the Continental Divide in the central part of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system passing over northern NM, in conjunction with a preceding cold front pushing south and westward through eastern NM produced moderate to heavy snow accumulations across central and northern NM during the night of November 24th into the morning of November 25th. The heaviest snow accumulations of 5 to 11 inches focused over the northern mountain areas, with accumulations of 3 to 5 inches at lower elevations in Rio Rancho, the East Mountain Communities, Santa Fe and Taos. A few lower elevation localities received 5 to 8 inches, including Dulce, Cuba, and Abiquiu. Difficult to locally severe driving conditions were also reported, highlighted by two rollover crashes along I-40 within the Tijeras Canyon to Sedillo Hill stretch just east of Albuquerque. Snow cleared out of the area by the late morning of November 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported 6 inches of snow in the town of Dulce. Three inches was also reported near El Vado Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Many severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening across southeast New Mexico and the Permian Basin. These thunderstorms developed within strong mid-level flow aloft as a trough passed across the Midwest and a cold front sagged into the region. Damaging wind gusts and large hail were observed with thunderstorms across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous power poles were reported snapped and outbuildings were severely damaged. Time is estimated from radar. Wind speeds were estimated to be approximately 100 mph. Property damage cost is an estimation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple power lines were blown down at the intersection of North Grimes and Cottonwood Lane. Wind speeds were estimated to be approximately 90 mph. Property damage cost is an estimation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: October continued the run of below normal rainfall for southwest New Mexico  which kept Severe(D2) to Exceptional(D4) drought conditions going through the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme(D3) drought conditions continued through the month across the entire zone.  The Cooperative Observer in Cliff reported 0.53 inches of precipitation which was 0.42 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large trough across the western CONUS helped initiate thunderstorms across the western higher elevations. Locally strong to severe winds were observed with these thunderstorms.
Another month with most areas receiving below normal monsoon season precipitation lead to all areas of southern New Mexico being put into Severe(D2) to Exceptional(D4) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone went from Severe(D2) drought to Extreme(D3) drought.  The Cooperative Observer in Cliff received 2.22 inches of precipitation which was 0.23 inches above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another month with most areas receiving below normal monsoon season precipitation lead to all areas of southern New Mexico being put into Severe(D2) to Exceptional(D4) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The zone started out with Severe(D2) drought in the west and Moderate(D1) drought east.  By the end of the month, the west was in Extreme(D3) drought and the east was in Severe(D2) drought. The White Signal Cooperative Observer reported 1.03 inches of precipitation which was 0.57 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another month with most areas receiving below normal monsoon season precipitation lead to all areas of southern New Mexico being put into Severe(D2) to Exceptional(D4) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The zone started with Severe(D2) drought across the zone, by the end of the month the west was in Exceptional(D4) drought and Extreme(D3) drought in the east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Many severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening across southeast New Mexico and the Permian Basin. These thunderstorms developed within strong mid-level flow aloft as a trough passed across the Midwest and a cold front sagged into the region. Damaging wind gusts and large hail were observed with thunderstorms across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn was reported collapsed due to strong winds. Wind speeds are estimated to be approximately 90 mph. Property damage cost is an estimation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough near the Four Corners with an accompanying sub-tropical jet streak helped initiate afternoon thunderstorms across much of southeast New Mexico and far west Texas. Several strong to severe thunderstorms developed across this region. One particular thunderstorm developed into a left-moving supercell. This supercell produced a long-track swath of intense straight-line winds in Loving and Lea Counties causing large amounts of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lea County Emergency Manager reports multiple downed power poles along Highway 18 in Hobbs. The exact locations and time is estimated from radar. Property damage cost is an estimation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms, each weaker but colder than the one before passed over New Mexico from November 30th to December 2nd. The first system was well defined and passed west to east mainly along I-40 producing several inches up to a foot at elevations above seven thousand feet by the morning of November 30th. The most severe impacts were travel related on the I-40 and I-25 corridors through the East Mountain Communities and Santa Fe. Snow accumulations of 4 to 10 inches in these areas produced car accidents along I-40 near Rancho Verde and the shutdown of I-25 from Santa Fe to Glorieta Pass. The second and third storm system were much weaker and only produced additional snowfall at higher elevations with no further impacts being reported on December 1st and 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cibola County Emergency Manager reported 13 inches of snow just west of Mt. Taylor. Heavy snow lead to impacts to travel along I-40 from Thoreau to Gallup as reported on social media. Four to seven inches of snow were also reported along the Continental Divide in the central part of the zone. This report is a carryover for an even that started on November 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant surge of low level moisture from the Gulf of Mexico streamed into eastern and central NM on December 13th ahead of an approaching upper level storm system from the Pacific Northwest. Fog, freezing fog, and snow spread over portions of the highlands adjacent to the Sangre de Cristo and Sandia/Manzano Mountains. Snow packed and icy roads developed along the East Slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains forecast zone, causing travel impacts and a jackknifed semi truck near Mora on December 13th. Snow expanded across the northern mountains as the upper low crossed over AZ and into western NM during the night of the 13th into the morning of the 14th. This continued through the day of December 14th with significant snow accumulations of 10 to 21 inches atop the peaks of the northern mountains of NM. Snow reached down the mid elevations, including Santa Fe through Glorieta Pass and down through the East Mountain Communities during the morning of the 14th. Snow packed and icy roads causing travel impacts resulting in severe winter driving conditions were reported in these areas from Santa Fe to Glorieta to Edgewood and Moriarty and to Clines Corners by the NM Department of Transportation. The rain to snow transition zone stretched northeastward along a line from near Clines Corners to Clayton during December 14th. Conditions steadily improved west to east across the state during the afternoon of December 14th with the storm moving into TX by the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were two reports from a storm spotter and a CoCoRaHS observer of 8 inches of snow, one in Raton and the other in Maxwell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few winter storms moved across the state during December providing beneficial rain and mountain snow to the state. However, this only resulted in the removal of extreme drought conditions across parts of north central New Mexico and removal of severe drought conditions across parts of central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions persisted across most of the zone. However, beneficial snowfall from several winter storms allowed severe drought conditions be eliminated in the northeast part of the zone.
