EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms, each weaker but colder than the one before passed over New Mexico from November 30th to December 2nd. The first system was well defined and passed west to east mainly along I-40 producing several inches up to a foot at elevations above seven thousand feet by the morning of November 30th. The most severe impacts were travel related on the I-40 and I-25 corridors through the East Mountain Communities and Santa Fe. Snow accumulations of 4 to 10 inches in these areas produced car accidents along I-40 near Rancho Verde and the shutdown of I-25 from Santa Fe to Glorieta Pass. The second and third storm system were much weaker and only produced additional snowfall at higher elevations with no further impacts being reported on December 1st and 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer reported 12.3 inches in the far northwestern corner of the zone by the morning of December 2nd. Amounts of nine to ten inches were also observed in southern portions of the zone and in Los Alamos. Heavy snow also closed portion of the main road through Los Alamos. This is a carryover report for an even that started on November 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant surge of low level moisture from the Gulf of Mexico streamed into eastern and central NM on December 13th ahead of an approaching upper level storm system from the Pacific Northwest. Fog, freezing fog, and snow spread over portions of the highlands adjacent to the Sangre de Cristo and Sandia/Manzano Mountains. Snow packed and icy roads developed along the East Slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains forecast zone, causing travel impacts and a jackknifed semi truck near Mora on December 13th. Snow expanded across the northern mountains as the upper low crossed over AZ and into western NM during the night of the 13th into the morning of the 14th. This continued through the day of December 14th with significant snow accumulations of 10 to 21 inches atop the peaks of the northern mountains of NM. Snow reached down the mid elevations, including Santa Fe through Glorieta Pass and down through the East Mountain Communities during the morning of the 14th. Snow packed and icy roads causing travel impacts resulting in severe winter driving conditions were reported in these areas from Santa Fe to Glorieta to Edgewood and Moriarty and to Clines Corners by the NM Department of Transportation. The rain to snow transition zone stretched northeastward along a line from near Clines Corners to Clayton during December 14th. Conditions steadily improved west to east across the state during the afternoon of December 14th with the storm moving into TX by the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter reported 6 inches of snow along U.S. Highway 412 a few miles east of the Yates Highway intersection. Lower amounts of 2 to 2.5 inches were reported in Clayton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively warm winter storm system moved across New Mexico December 22nd through the 24th. The system provided widespread valley rain and mountain snow with only the high mountain peaks seeing high snow accumulations. Ski Santa Fe reported 13 inches of snow. Meanwhile, a line of storms developed across south-central New Mexico with strong winds causing a tree branch to fall on a house in Ruidoso. This line of storms continued eastward and produced a severe wind gust in Clovis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds blew down large tree branches onto a house. Wind speed is an estimate based on the large tree branch broken causing damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong portion of the jetstream passed over NM on December 2nd behind an exiting winter storm event. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cannon AFB ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds in the upper levels of the atmosphere remained over New Mexico December 3rd. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant surge of low level moisture from the Gulf of Mexico streamed into eastern and central NM on December 13th ahead of an approaching upper level storm system from the Pacific Northwest. Fog, freezing fog, and snow spread over portions of the highlands adjacent to the Sangre de Cristo and Sandia/Manzano Mountains. Snow packed and icy roads developed along the East Slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains forecast zone, causing travel impacts and a jackknifed semi truck near Mora on December 13th. Snow expanded across the northern mountains as the upper low crossed over AZ and into western NM during the night of the 13th into the morning of the 14th. This continued through the day of December 14th with significant snow accumulations of 10 to 21 inches atop the peaks of the northern mountains of NM. Snow reached down the mid elevations, including Santa Fe through Glorieta Pass and down through the East Mountain Communities during the morning of the 14th. Snow packed and icy roads causing travel impacts resulting in severe winter driving conditions were reported in these areas from Santa Fe to Glorieta to Edgewood and Moriarty and to Clines Corners by the NM Department of Transportation. The rain to snow transition zone stretched northeastward along a line from near Clines Corners to Clayton during December 14th. Conditions steadily improved west to east across the state during the afternoon of December 14th with the storm moving into TX by the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were two reports of by trained spotters of 3 to 6 inches in Roy. Another spotter reported 3.5 inches just west of Mills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two sequential winter storm systems rolled through northern and central New Mexico on February 13, 2023 and February 14, 2023. Heavy snow was reported across much of the northern mountains of New Mexico, as well as a few isolated spots in and around the Santa Fe and Albuquerque metro areas by the morning of February 14, 2023. Snow accumulations reaching 10 to 11 inches were reported atop the Sangre de Cristo Mountains, while severe winter driving conditions were reported on U.S. Highway 64 in the Tusas Mountains. Strong westerly winds filled in quickly as the main upper level low exited the area during the morning of February 14, 2023. High winds with peak gusts of 60 to 70 mph were reported through the southwest mountains, Sandia and Manzano Mountains, and over a large area of the eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS station along I-40 reported 7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second winter storm to bring widespread high wind damages and a snow squall to northern and central NM within the span of a week arrived on February 26, 2023. Incredibly strong winds impacted near all areas of the state with the exception of the northwestern quarter of New Mexico. Peak gusts of 60 to 70 mph were common, with the strongest gusts reaching 80 to 95 mph recorded near Magdalena, the Estancia Valley, Tucumcari, Roswell, and Clovis. Wind damage in the form of downed trees and power polls were reported in Albuquerque, Clovis, Roswell, and Hagerman. High winds continued overnight into the morning of February 27, 2023 for an isolated area in western Guadalupe County. Meanwhile, winter weather impacted parts of western and central NM in the form of a snow squall that brought a brief period of strong gusty winds and near zero visibility from blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mountainair RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally cold upper level winter storm system impacted northern and central NM on February 15, 2023. Widespread heavy snow was reported across western, northern, and central NM. Impacts began late in the day on February 14, 2023, peaking in intensity during the morning of February 15, 2023 before the storm exited late in the day. Snow accumulations of over a foot up to 15 inches were reported along the Continental Divide and northern mountain peaks. Blowing snow reducing visibility and snow packed roads caused reports of severe winter travel and forced some roads to be closed. These included I-25 at Raton Pass and U.S. Highway 64 from Raton to Clayton. Strong winds were also present with this storm system, although less pronounced compared to the prior storm system. A lone high wind report at the Roswell Airport was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow impacted a small portion of the zone in Cuba where a trained spotter measured 6 inches of snow. There was another report of 4 inches in Cuba with 2.1 inches at Torreon Navajo Mission.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low in combination with a strong surface cold front brought moderate to heavy snow accumulations. Accumulations ranging from 4 to 7 inches fell during the day of February 7, 2023 as the upper low crossed west to east over southern New Mexico, with easterly upslope flow behind the cold front enhancing snowfall rates. This was especially true during the evening hours through the Sandia and Manzano Mountains. The heaviest accumulations focused within the east mountain communities along the Sandia and Manzano Mountains, and in Magdalena in the San Agustin Plains in western Socorro County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow impacted the zone where a trained spotter near Sandia Park reported 7 inches of snow. There were various other reports of 3 to 7 inches of snow were reported throughout the east mountain communities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: February saw more wetter than normal conditions across the western half of NM with drier than normal conditions persistent through much of the eastern plains of NM. There was very little change in the drought conditions however. By month's end, 16 percent of the state was in severe to extreme drought, of which about 3 percent was extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions persisted through a portion of the zone during February.

Strong westerly winds filled in quickly as the main upper level low exited the area during the morning of February 14, 2023. High winds with peak gusts of 60 to 70 mph were reported through the southwest mountains, Sandia and Manzano Mountains, and over a large area of the eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS station reported 6.3 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two sequential winter storm systems rolled through northern and central New Mexico on February 13, 2023 and February 14, 2023. Heavy snow was reported across much of the northern mountains of New Mexico, as well as a few isolated spots in and around the Santa Fe and Albuquerque metro areas by the morning of February 14, 2023. Snow accumulations reaching 10 to 11 inches were reported atop the Sangre de Cristo Mountains, while severe winter driving conditions were reported on U.S. Highway 64 in the Tusas Mountains. Strong westerly winds filled in quickly as the main upper level low exited the area during the morning of February 14, 2023. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snotel reported 11 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally cold upper level winter storm system impacted northern and central NM on February 15, 2023. Widespread heavy snow was reported across western, northern, and central NM. Impacts began late in the day on February 14, 2023, peaking in intensity during the morning of February 15, 2023 before the storm exited late in the day. Snow accumulations of over a foot up to 15 inches were reported along the Continental Divide and northern mountain peaks. Blowing snow reducing visibility and snow packed roads caused reports of severe winter travel and forced some roads to be closed. These included I-25 at Raton Pass and U.S. Highway 64 from Raton to Clayton. Strong winds were also present with this storm system, although less pronounced compared to the prior storm system. A lone high wind report at the Roswell Airport was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Highway 64 was closed from Raton to Clayton due to snow packed and icy roads along with reduced visibility from blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second winter storm to bring widespread high wind damages and a snow squall to northern and central NM within the span of a week arrived on February 26, 2023. Incredibly strong winds impacted near all areas of the state with the exception of the northwestern quarter of New Mexico. Peak gusts of 60 to 70 mph were common, with the strongest gusts reaching 80 to 95 mph recorded near Magdalena, the Estancia Valley, Tucumcari, Roswell, and Clovis. Wind damage in the form of downed trees and power polls were reported in Albuquerque, Clovis, Roswell, and Hagerman. High winds continued overnight into the morning of February 27, 2023 for an isolated area in western Guadalupe County. Meanwhile, winter weather impacted parts of western and central NM in the form of a snow squall that brought a brief period of strong gusty winds and near zero visibility from blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported wind damages that includes the Curry County Fairgrounds, incurring 2 million dollars of damage. Retractable awning, swamp cooler, rain gutters, portable ticket booths, 40ft rolling chain link gate to the parking lot. Roof damage to the Roy Walker Recreation Center. The city of Clovis incurred about 1 million dollars of damage to the roof of a gym and several smaller facilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving upper level disturbance passing southward along the Rocky Mountains brought a potent cold front that surged south through eastern NM. High winds and light to moderate snow accompanied this storm system. High wind gusts greater than 60 mph were observed at Raton and through portions of Union County. The combination of strong winds and blowing snow also caused several road closures and crashes along U.S. Highway 64 from Raton to Clayton, and along I-40 in the Central Highlands. An isolated snowfall accumulation of 5 inches in DeBaca County were reported as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Highway 64 closed due to near whiteout conditions causing blowing snow and low visibility all along the highway between Raton and Clayton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally cold upper level winter storm system impacted northern and central NM on February 15, 2023. Widespread heavy snow was reported across western, northern, and central NM. Impacts began late in the day on February 14, 2023, peaking in intensity during the morning of February 15, 2023 before the storm exited late in the day. Snow accumulations of over a foot up to 15 inches were reported along the Continental Divide and northern mountain peaks. Blowing snow reducing visibility and snow packed roads caused reports of severe winter travel and forced some roads to be closed. These included I-25 at Raton Pass and U.S. Highway 64 from Raton to Clayton. Strong winds were also present with this storm system, although less pronounced compared to the prior storm system. A lone high wind report at the Roswell Airport was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow impacted the higher terrain of the southern Sangre de Cristo Mountains where there was a public report of 22 inches of snow near Santa Fe Ski. There were also various other reports of 8 to 10 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two sequential winter storm systems rolled through northern and central New Mexico on February 13, 2023 and February 14, 2023. CZ_NAME: JOHNSON AND BARTLETT MESAS INCLUDING RATON PASS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A modified Arctic airmass pushed southward into eastern NM January 31, 2023 and into February 1, 2023. Freezing fog developed behind the cold front, focusing along the New Mexico and Colorado border from Raton to Clayton. While light icing was recorded by the Raton Municipal Airport ASOS, heavier ice accretions were reported by a weather observer over rural areas of Colfax and Union Counties on the morning of February 1, 2023. Power outages in this area resulted from the ice accretion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power outages were reported within rural Colfax County with 2 inches of icing also being reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: February saw more wetter than normal conditions across the western half of NM with drier than normal conditions persistent through much of the eastern plains of NM. There was very little change in the drought conditions however. By month's end, 16 percent of the state was in severe to extreme drought, of which about 3 percent was extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from October 2022 persisted through February 2023.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: February saw more wetter than normal conditions across the western half of NM with drier than normal conditions persistent through much of the eastern plains of NM. There was very little change in the drought conditions however. By month's end, 16 percent of the state was in severe to extreme drought, of which about 3 percent was extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions from October 2022 continued through March 2023. A very small area of exceptional drought between Clayton and Sedan persisted through February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally cold upper level winter storm system impacted northern and central NM on February 15, 2023. Widespread heavy snow was reported across western, northern, and central NM. Impacts began late in the day on February 14, 2023, peaking in intensity during the morning of February 15, 2023 before the storm exited late in the day. Snow accumulations of over a foot up to 15 inches were reported along the Continental Divide and northern mountain peaks. Blowing snow reducing visibility and snow packed roads caused reports of severe winter travel and forced some roads to be closed. These included I-25 at Raton Pass and U.S. Highway 64 from Raton to Clayton. Strong winds were also present with this storm system, although less pronounced compared to the prior storm system. A lone high wind report at the Roswell Airport was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow impacted the northwestern section of the zone where there was a public report of 8 inches of snow just north of Glorieta, NM.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second winter storm to bring widespread high wind damages and a snow squall to northern and central NM within the span of a week arrived on February 26, 2023. Incredibly strong winds impacted near all areas of the state with the exception of the northwestern quarter of New Mexico. Peak gusts of 60 to 70 mph were common, with the strongest gusts reaching 80 to 95 mph recorded near Magdalena, the Estancia Valley, Tucumcari, Roswell, and Clovis. Wind damage in the form of downed trees and power polls were reported in Albuquerque, Clovis, Roswell, and Hagerman. High winds continued overnight into the morning of February 27, 2023 for an isolated area in western Guadalupe County. Meanwhile, winter weather impacted parts of western and central NM in the form of a snow squall that brought a brief period of strong gusty winds and near zero visibility from blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A private weather station KNMRAINS3 measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous late-season severe weather setup developed across the southern Great Plains during the start of October. A large slow-moving trough across the Four Corners combined with very rich low-level moisture to produce widespread severe weather and localized flash flooding, especially across portions of southeast New Mexico and west Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters starting to run across US Highway 285.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A digging trough across northern Mexico produced sufficient instability and wind shear for scattered supercells across portions of far west Texas and southeast New Mexico. Large hail was observed with a few of these thunderstorms.

A large trough over the Central Rocky Mountains brought westerly high winds to the Guadalupe and Delaware Mountains.

A shortwave trough near the Four Corners with an accompanying sub-tropical jet streak helped initiate afternoon thunderstorms across much of southeast New Mexico and far west Texas. Several strong to severe thunderstorms developed across this region. One particular thunderstorm developed into a left-moving supercell. This supercell produced a long-track swath of intense straight-line winds in Loving and Lea Counties causing large amounts of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple overturned irrigation pivots were found. A storm survey team found winds estimated to be 110 MPH. Property damage cost is an estimation.
